text: AQUITAINE, the ancient name of Guienne, a ci-devant province of France, now forming the two departments of Gironde, and of Lot and Garonne. It formerly belonged to the kings of England, as descendants of William the Conqueror. Jersey and Guernsey are all that now belong to the king of Great Britain, of the ancient dominions of Aquitaine and Normandy.
ARA, the name of two rivers in Spain; one in Catalonia, a branch of the Segri, the other in Arragon, a branch of the Cinca.

ARAASOJAVA, or Guarasojava, a mountain of Brazil, in the prov. of San Paulo, rising to the alt. of 1,000 ft. above the Rio Ypanema, to the NW of San João de Ypanema. It is celebrated for its gigantic rocks of magnetic ironstone; and extensive smelting-works were established at San João with the hope of entirely supplying Brazil with native iron; but the expenses have hitherto exceeded any revenue drawn from them.—Spix's Brazil, vol. ii. pp. 46-52.—Com. Tariff of Brazil, p. 42.
ARABA, a river of Persia, which falls into the Arabian gulf, in N lat. 25° 30', E long. 65° 40'.

ARABAH (WADI), a great sandy valley of Syria and Arabia, extending from the southern extremity of the Dead sea to within a few miles of the head of the gulf of Akabah. This extraordinary region, called towards the N, El Ghor, and towards the S, El Arabah, was first brought to light in modern times by Burckhardt. Abulfeda indeed mentions it in the 14th century; and it must of course have been known to the Crusaders, who for a time had possession of Aila or Elath; though it is not mentioned by any of the writers of that age. The exact length of the valley cannot well be ascertained, because the latitude of the southern extremity of the Dead sea has never been determined. Assuming this to be 31° 15'—which is probably not far from the truth-we have between this point and Akabah, in lat. 29° 31', an interval of 104 minutes of latitude, or about 120 English miles. With this estimate, the distances as given by travellers very nearly accord. The valley has a general direction from N by E to S by W, and is from 3 m. to 10 m. broad. It is covered throughout with sand, apparently driven up by the winds from the Red sea, and a ridge of sand-hills runs quite across it, at the distance of a few miles from the Dead sea. Through this great valley Burckhardt supposes that the Jordan once rolled its flood, and emptied itself into the gulf of Akabah, before the great catastrophe which destroyed Sodom and Gomorrah, and converted the fertile plain, in which they stood, into a gloomy reservoir of dead and bitter waters; but both Schubert and Lieut. Symonds found the level of the Dead sea to be at least 1,312 ft. below the level of the Mediterranean, which is itself 31 ft. lower than the Red sea, consequently it is impossible that the Jordan could ever have flowed through the Wadi Arabah. Both Rüppell and Laborde inform us that all along the shore, at the northern extremity of the gulf of Akabah, where the valley El Arabah meets it, on digging holes a foot deep in the sand, at the time of ebb, they are constantly filled with the purest water. In this way the wants of the fortress at the head of the gulf are supplied. "The existence of this water," says Ruppell, "can be accounted for in no other way, than by supposing a very copious filtration of the water which collects in Wadi Arabah, through the layer of sand which covers the granite formation beneath;" and it has been argued that this water must percolate from the Dead sea beneath the rocky sands. The sweetness of this water seems to suggest a different source; and in fact the Wadi rises from the Dead sea to the transverse ridge called El Sathe or 'the Roof,' which forms the water-shed between the waters flowing N towards the Dead sea, and S to the gulf of Akabah: see the latter article. The Ghor, throughout its whole extent, has commonly a hard stony bottom, and is of course for the most part a desert; but patches of softer material, sand with a little soil upon it, here and there occur, especially where there is a depression of the surface; and in winter, when the torrents from the neighbouring mountains produce a copious supply of water, a few shrubs spring up in these patches, and along the banks of the streams, affording pasturage to the sheep and goats of Bedouin tribes which then encamp here. Towards the southern extremity, vegetation is more frequent in the valley; and Ruppell, in April, 1822, found it here shaded with bushes and covered with luxuriant pasturage. Boulders and rounded stones of red and white granite, porphyry, basalt, sandstone, and lime, are in many parts scattered over the surface. The range of mountains on the western side is in general much lower and narrower than those on the eastern; and they appear never to have been the site of towns or cities. The descent from these mountains near Akabah is estimated by Ruppell at 1,500 French ft. The eastern chain is much higher and broader, and is often interrupted by deep wadis running down from the eastern plateau into the great valley. Near the head of the gulf of Akabah, the same traveller estimates their alt. at 3,000 French ft. The breadth of these mountains, between the upper desert and the Ghor, is in general from 8 to 12 hours of distance, or from 20 to 30 m. The tract which here skirts them on the E, is the vast and stony plain of the Arabian desert. Its level is much higher than that of the Ghor, so that the mountains appear from it only as low hills.

ARABAH (WADI), a valley of Central Egypt, extending from the plateau of Abu Rich, between the Nile and the Red sea, in a curved direction from WSW to ENE, a little to the S of the parallel of 29°. ARA MM. Figari and Husson have given a detailed account of their exploratory journey through this valley in the Bulletin de la Societé de Geographie for 1846. They estimate its length at about 28 leagues, and its mean breadth at 6 leagues. The range of mountains which bound it on the W, they call Jebel-Kolaïl; that to the E, Jelaleh-Jeblieh on its southern part, and Jelaleh-Baharyeh as it approaches the Red sea. The surface of the valley is covered with small hills and mounds of tertiary formation, and the patches of soil consist of a calcareous clay. In the rainy season, numerous streams descend from the ranges on both sides; and when the dry season returns the valley becomes covered with a rich floral vegetation; whence, according to our travellers, its name Wadi Rahabieh, or Wadi A'rabah, i. e., 'the Valley of Spring.'
ARABAN, a town of Asiatic Turkey, on the Khabur, 76 m. SE of Orfa, in N lat. 36° 20', E long. 40°.
ARABANATE, a large lake of Peru, in the prov. of Mainas, which communicates with the Guallaga. This lake abounds with turtle.

ARABAT, a village of the Crimea, in the district of Simferopol, on the coast of the sea of Azof, in N lat. 45° 24', E long. 35° 15'. There is a fort here at the head of the isthmus between the Sivash and the sea of Azof, which defends the entrance into the Crimean peninsula.
ARABAZARI, a town of Karamania, 16 m. NE of Alamek.

ARABGIR, a town of Turkish Armenia, between Egin and Keban-Maden, from both of which places it is about 10 hours or 30 m. distant. It contains 6,000 houses, of which 4,800 belong to Mahommedans; and there are said to be above 1,000 looms in the place occupied in weaving cotton-goods from English yarn. It is situated on a plateau, in the midst of a forest of fruit-trees, among which the white mulberry is the most common. The climate is reckoned severe, and snow falls in considerable quantity during winter. It is about 20 m. distant from the Euphrates; and 15 caravan-days, or 270 m., from Aleppo.
ARAB-HISSAR, a town of Anatolia, 38 m. NW of Mogla. Near it are the ruins of an ancient city.

ARABIA. An extensive region, which in position and physical characteristics might be considered as belonging to Africa, but is regarded by most geographers as belonging to Asia, or as a nearly detached peninsular extension of the SW extremity of the latter conti nent, with which it is connected by a sandy isthmus stretching between the N end of the gulf of Akabah and the head of the Persian gulf. Boundaries and Extent. Arabia is bounded on the N by part of Syria, and the Euphrates; on the E by the Chaldean mountains, the Persian gulf, and the gulf of Oman; on the S by the Indian ocean, and the Straits of Babel-Mandeb; and on the W by the Red sea, and the gulf and the isthmus of Suez, which divides it from Abyssinia, Nubia, and Egypt. Its northern boundaries are somewhat general and undefined: the northern deserts of Arabia, and the southern deserts of Syria and Irak-Arabi having no distinct line of demarcation physical or political. A line drawn from the head of the Persian gulf to the head of the gulf of Akabah would exclude the greater part of Arabia Petræa. A line drawn from the same point on the E to the SE corner of the Mediterranean, at El Arish, and from the latter point to the head of the gulf of Suez, has been adopted by some geographers; while others, from the time of Xenophon, assign to A. the whole country lying to the S of the Euphrates. Burckhardt defines the northern frontier by a line running from Suez, at the head of the gulf of that name, to the Mediterranean near El Arish; thence passing along the southern border of Palestine and the Dead sea; thence winding through the Syrian desert to Palmyra; and thence crossing to the Euphrates, which it strikes at Anah. Taking the latter boundary on the N, A. lies between the parallels of 12° 40' and 34° N, but the country to the N of the 30th parallel is often treated in connexion with the Asiatic provinces of the Turkish empire and Persia. Its E and W limits are between the extreme meridional points of 59° 40' and 32° 10' E.—Its greatest length from Suez to Ras-al-Had on the sea of Oman, along a line running WNW, is 1,440 geographical or 1,660 English m.; along the 22d parallel it has an extent of 1,200 English m. From the straits of Bab-el-Mandeb to the Euphrates, along the meridian of 43° E, is 1,400 m.—Its superficial area has been estimated by Templemann at 1,182,000 sq. m.; by Reichard, at 1,005,727 sq. m.; by the writer of the article A. in Macculloch's Geog. Dict., as measured on D'Anville's map, at about 1,100,000 sq. m.; by Balbi at 834,000 sq. m. These admeasurements are all mere approximations of course; but we may perhaps roughly estimate the area of this country at 1,000,000 sq. m. Names.] The most ancient name under which A. has been known is that of Kedem or 'the East;' and its inhabitants still denominate themselves Beni Kedem or 'Sons of the East.' It was called Eretz Kedem, or 'the Land of the East,' in relation to Palestine; and Arabah, or 'the Desert,' a most appropriate epithet, was given to the western division particularly by Moses, which appellation, in process of time, was applied to the whole region. The Arabah of Moses corresponded to A. Petræa, or the country of the modern Bedouins; and Kedem to A. Deserta and A. Felix. The modern Arabian historians, however, reject this derivation of the name, and maintain that both the country and their language received their names from Yarab, the son of Joktan, the supposed founder of the kingdom of Yemen. This country was also called Cush, from the eldest son of Ham, whose descendants peopled a great part of A., and the form Cushatha is used by several Syriac writers. The modern Arabians denominate their country Jezirat-el-Arab, i. e., 'the Peninsula of the Arabs;' or Belad-el-Arab, 'the Land of the Arabs;' whilst the Turks and Persians call it Arabistan, 'the Country of the Arabs.' By the Greek and Roman writers, all the Arabian tribes, from Mecca to the Euphrates, were confounded under the general appellation of Saracens; which epithet was afterwards extended by Christian writers to the whole of the races inhabiting the peninsula, whether descended from Joktan, Cush, or Ishmael; and even to all the various nations who professed their belief in the divine mission of the prophet of Mecca, or owned the sway of his successors, the khalifs of Bagdad, Cordova, or Egypt. The name Saraceni, however, was originally used by Pliny and Ptolemy in a restricted sense, and applied by these writers to the erratic tribes on the frontiers of Palestine and Egypt. Ammianus Marcellinus and Procopius afterwards applied it in a more enlarged sense to all the tribes, from the Euphrates to the frontiers of Egypt. This name seems easily deducible from the Arabic Sarra, or Sahara, 'the Waste;' hence Saharakiin, or Sarakiin, Saracens, or 'Inhabitants of the Waste, or Desert.' This appears a more natural etymology than that proposed by some—as Jerome and others—who derive the name Saracens from Sarah, the wife of Abraham, the Ishmaelites being in fact not her descendants, but those of Hagar; or that of Scaliger, who derives it from Sarak, 'he stole privately.' The Arabs are not thieves, but robbers; and it is scarcely credible, as Voetius remarks, that a whole nation would have allowed itself to be denominated a nation of thieves or robbers, or, what is still more improbable, have called themselves such. Another etymology of the name Saracens, as if it signified 'Easterlings,' from Sharkiin, 'an eastern man,' from Sharki, 'the East,' is more plausible, and has been adopted by Pococke, Des Langles, and many others. It is true that the African Arabs, dwelling to the east of the Nile, are called Sharkiin, and their country Belad-el-Sharkieh, or 'the Eastern country;' but this is a mere modern appellation, originating in the conquest of Northern Africa by the Arabs, in the first century of the Hejira, when the appellation of El Garbia, or 'the West,' was given first to the western part of Egypt, and then to the whole of Africa which lay to the west of Egypt, and even to the western part of Spain, as appears from the name Algarva, still remaining a modern geographical appellation for the SW part of Portugal. In consequence of this new name applied to Western Africa, the African Arabs came to be distinguished from the Asiatic Arabs, by the name of Mogrebbins, or inhabitants of El Moghrib, or 'the West,' in distinction from the Sharkiin, or 'Eastern Arabs. The opinion of Dr. Wells, that the appellation Saracens is derived from Sabtecha, the son of Cush, the son of Ham, whose descendants, called Sabtaceni by the Greeks, were, as he fancies, the real Saracens-Saraceni being a corruption of Sabtaceni—is the least tenable of all the conjectural etymons which have been devised for the appellation; and has in fact no foundation whatever but his fanciful supposition of an error in orthography by the Greeks and Romans, in writing Saraceni for Sabtaceni. This hypothesis, besides, restricts the name of Saracens to one branch only, and that a very obscure one, of the Arabian people, namely, to a Cushite tribe, of which nothing is known but the name. In modern times the appellation Saracens has become obsolete; and that of Bedouins from the Arabic Bedowee, or Badwi, an appellation denoting 'Inhabitants of the desert,' has been substituted in its place. Progressive Geography and Divisions.] Moses has given us some information respecting the northwestern parts of the A. peninsula, with which he had opportunities of becoming acquainted while sojourning as an exile in the land of Midian, and when conducting his people through the deserts of A. Petræa. The Greeks and Romans seldom entered a country but on military expeditions, and Arabia defied all their armies. Herodotus says that the Arabs alone, of all the nations of Asia—that is of Western Asia—declined the yoke of Darius Hystaspes; and Cambyses, as a preliminary to attacking Egypt, found it necessary to propitiate the friendship of the Arabs. Alexander the Great died while on the eve of attempting the circumnavigation of A., and his successors were repulsed in all their attacks upon the Nabathæi, whose capital was Petra. In the reign of Augustus, when Syria had become a Roman prov., Ælius Gallus, assisted by the king of Petra, led an expedition into A. Felix, which reached Marsyabæ, the cap. of the Sabæans; but was ultimately forced by the climate to retire across the Red sea into Egypt. We cannot therefore expect to find many geographical notices of this country in ancient writers. Strabo, and the early Greek geographers, divide the country into A. Felix, or 'A. the Happy,' and A. Deserta, or 'A. the Desert.' Ptolemy added the third division of A. Petræa, so named from its cap. Petra. A. Felix comprised the fertile, habitable, peninsular regions of the S. A. Deserta extended on the N and E over the whole expanse of the Syrian desert, as far as the Euphrates, which separated it from Mesopotamia. A. Petræa comprised the country to the S of the Dead sea, between Palestine and Egypt, and along the northern extremity of the Red sea. The writers of the middle ages employ the terms A. Prima, A. Secunda, and A. Tertia; but with great misapprehension of the actual features of the country, and relative position of localities. By Oriental writers, A. is divided into five provinces: viz., Yemen, Hejaz or Hedjaz, Tehama, Nejd or Nejed, and Yemama. In this division of the country — which prevailed before the time of Ptolemy—A. Petræa seems to have been assigned partly to Egypt and partly to Syria; while the greater part of A. Deserta was known as the Desert of Syria. Some Oriental writers represent Tehama, Nejd, and Yemama as subdivisions of Hejaz; while some add a sixth prov. in Bahrein, which others regard as a portion of Irak-Arabi. The modern native divisions are as follows: I. The BAHR-EL-TOUR SINAI, or Wuste-el-Tyh, which comprises the small peninsula between the Mediterranean and the two northern arms of the Red sea, and is nearly identical with the A. Petræa of Ptolemy. It is frequently denominated by modern geographers the Peninsula of Sinai, and can hardly be regarded as a political division of A. II. Hejaz lies to the S of the above, and stretches along the E shore of the Red sea to the parallel of 19°. The name signifies 'the Land of Pilgrimage,' and has been given to it from its containing the cities of Mecca and Medina, and thus constituting the Holy Land of Mahommedan pilgrims. It is a strip of desert country, consisting of sandy plains towards the shore, and rocky hills in the interior, and bounded eastward by a lofty range of mountains. It acknowledges the sway rather of the pasha of Egypt than of the Porte; but has been more fully described by the Arabian geographers than any other part of their country. The Beled-el- Haram, or Holy Land Proper of A. occupies the centre of this district, and was under the peculiar jurisdiction of the sheriff of Mecca. Its chief seaports are Jidda, which may be considered as the port of Mecca; and Yembo, which is the port of Medina. The Howeitat Arabs have possession of the coast from about the 25th parallel northwards. III. YEMEN runs along the remaining part of the coast of the Red sea, to the straits of Bab-el- Mandeb. On the E it has the district of Hadramaut. It comprises the finest and most fertile portion of A., and is subdivided into several minor states, among which are Abu-Arisch, Sana, and Aden. The stronghold or port of Aden is now in the possession of Britain. IV. HADRAMAUT forms the great S division of A. It stretches along the Indian ocean from long. 45° to 56° 30', and runs far into the interior. With the exception of a strip along its coast, little is known of this region. The mountainous district of Mahra appears to belong partly to Hadramaut and partly to Oman. V. OMAN is bounded on the N by the Persian gulf; on the E and S by the Indian ocean; and on the W by Hadramaut and the great central desert of A. It is reckoned to extend six days' journey in breadth, going SW from the shores of the gulf of Oman; but its inland districts are little known. It is divided among several petty chiefs, the most powerful and best known of whom is the imam of Muscat, who claims all the sea-coast to the entrance of the Persian gulf, including the Bahrein islands, with the northern coast of the gulf nearly to Scinde. The Gharah territory, along the coast of the Indian ocean, to the NE of Mahra, is nominally included in Oman; but both these districts are in the possession of independent tribes. VI. EL-Achsa, or Hadjar, extends along the W coast of the Persian gulf, between Oman and Irak-Arabi or the Euphrates river. The territory of Bahrein is included in this district; whose inland boundaries are defined by a mountainrange on the eastern frontiers of the great central district of A. called the Nejed. VII. NEJED or NEJD, the central, and the largest, but the least known district of A., has the Syro- Arabian or Aneizeh desert on the N; El-Achsa on the E; Hadramaut on the S; and Hejaz on the W. It is in the hands of the Wahabites. The greater part of its surface consists of elevated sandy deserts, amongst which are several oases, and fertile wadis. It has a thin population of wandering tribes. Between Nejed and Hadramaut lies an immense tract of desert country, called by the Bedouins Roba-el-Khaly, or 'the Abode of Emptiness.' Little is known of this frightful wilderness, which still appears an entire blank on our maps. VIII. EL-HAMAD, or the Syro - Arabian desert, stretches from Nejed northwards to the Euphrates. It is treated by some geographers as a part of Nejed; but the latter term is probably, politically at least, restricted to the country lying S of Jebel Shammar. The Syro-Arabian desert is chiefly in the hands of the Aneizeh Arabs. The control over the Arab tribes in the northern part of this district, and along both banks of the Euphrates, by means of sheikhs nominated at Bagdad, who are stationed here, and communicate with the sheikhs in the Desert. General physical features.] The Arabian peninsula is bordered, along the sea-coast from Suez, round by the straits of Bab-el-Mandeb, to the head of the Persian gulf, by a belt of level, sandy, but in many places fertile, ground, varying in breadth from two days' journey to a single mile, and distinguished as the Tehama, or 'Low land.' Towards the interior it presents, in general, a flat arid desert, rising into an elevated central table-land, which declines on the N towards the Syro-Arabian desert, and is interspersed with a few fertile spots, or oases, and some mountains of considerable height, among which are found vallies of delightful luxuriance. The contrast between the desolation of the desert, and the beauty and fertility of those vallies and oases, has given rise to conflicting representations of Arabian scenery. Thus while some authors have represented it as throughout the habitation of poverty and wretchedness, yielding only the scantiest support to its miserable inhabitants; others—who have been fortunate enough to visit the verdant hills of Yemen, and the spicy mountains of Hadramaut—have spoken in raptures of its fertility and salubrity, and represented A. as abounding not only in the necessaries but the luxuries of life. Both descriptions, in relation to particular districts, are correct; but neither of them are so if applied to the country generally. The title of Happy A., which was given in ancient times to its southern provinces, has been supposed by some to have originated only in the contrast between them and the surrounding desert; but to doubt the former riches of A. Felix is to deny the authority of all history; and, even at this day, many districts of A. are unsurpassed in fertility of soil and mildness of climate; it is still famed for its frankincense and spices; and Milton's learned taste and fine imagination was not deceived when he wrote the well-known lines,- Now gentle gales, Fanning their odoriferous wings, dispense Native perfumes, and whisper whence they stole Those balmy spoils: As when to them who sail Beyond the cape of Hope, and now are past Mozambic, off at sea north-east winds blow Sabean odours, from the spicy shore Of ARABY THE BLESS'D, with such delay Well-pleas'd they slack their course, and many a league Cheer'd with the grateful smell old Ocean smiles. Mountains.] The southern ramifications of the chain of Mount Lebanon traverse the deserts of the NW part of A., and spread over the whole triangular peninsula between the forks or northern gulfs of the Red sea. One of these, passing to the east of the Dead sea, prolongs itself to the Desert. Another, running south from the neighbourhood of Jerusalem, takes the name of Mount Seir, sends off a branch called Jebel-Hairas to the SW, towards the isthmus of Suez, joins Jebel-Tekrael, and in 30° 5' N lat. rejoins the eastern ramification of the Lebanon range, which, to the S of the Dead sea, takes the name of Shakar, and in its farther course southward that of Jebel-el-Akabah, and finally terminates in small hills along the Arabian gulf. Mount Tor, the Sinai and Horeb of the ancients, joins these to the NE of the gulf of Akabah. Towards N lat. 29° the surface begins to rise and form that immense plateau which comprises the centre of the peninsula, and which has no connexion with the ramifications of Mount Lebanon. The mountains approach it from the coast, and increase in height going to the south. Those which surround the Hejaz are more remarkable for their Persian government affects to exercise some steepness and extreme aridity than for their height. They are granitic, according to Seitzen; but according to Ali Bey, with the exception of Mount Arafat, they are composed of micaceous schist mixed with hornstone. To the SW the high upland of Arabia retreats from the Red sea, rising from it by terraces, which reach their highest elevation in the plateau of Yemen. In Hadramaut the plateau is less distant from the sea, and approaches it gradually in its progress towards the NE, till in Oman it is separated from the ocean only by a very narrow stripe. From Ras-al-Had, the most eastern projection of the peninsula, where the mountains resume their elevation-which they preserve as far as Cape Mussendom at the entrance of the Persian gulf-the plateau appears to recede from the sea-coast; and from the latter cape, where they diminish in height again, sandy plains are prolonged far into the interior of the country. In the interior, Jebel Shammar, which is traversed by the caravan-route from Bagdad to Medina, is said to rival Mount Lebanon in elevation and extent, and to vie with it in extensive forests and numerous villages. The Torik mountains run N and S, crossing the eastern part of the plateau. The chain of the Jebel-Kur runs parallel to the Shammar from E to W. The mountains of El-Arad, composed of limestone-rocks, very steep to the west but sloping gently to the east, run NE and SW through Nejed, and terminate at Ras Reccan on the Persian gulf. A caravan-route, which leads from El-Achsa to Mecca, runs sometimes at their feet, and sometimes along their sides. Coasts. The coasts of A. present enormous banks of coral, and frequently terminate in prodigiously steep shores; but in other places are so low that the tide flows far into the land. The Tehama, or flat sandy belt which forms the maritime district of A., bears every mark of having gradually emerged from the sea; and as the sea continues to recede, the Tehama extends its limits in proportion. The whole of the southern coast is a wall of naked rocks. In some places, the coasts are bordered with numerous but small and unimportant islands. In the Persian gulf, the Bahrein isles are the most considerable. The island of Socotra, in the Indian ocean, is regarded as belonging to Arabia. Rivers.] Notwithstanding its numerous mountains, there is no country so destitute of water as A. It has, in fact, no considerable river. The few streams which originate in the mountains often lose themselves in the sands: indeed, the rivers are merely a sort of occasional torrents commonly denominated wadis. The chief are the Obur, the Sehan, the Abbacy, the Zebid, and the Kebir, which run into the Red sea; and the Meitam, the Chabb, the Prim, and the Masora, which flow from SW to NE into the Indian ocean. The Aftan-which, in D'Anville's map, falls into the Persian gulf over against the Bahrein isles—is a mere torrent, which flows only after heavy rains. Hadji Khalfa, the Turkish geographer, informs us, that the Nejed, or interior of Arabia, contains some lakes; Strabo also mentions some lakes which are here formed by rivers. But it is impossible, in our present ignorance of the geography of the interior, to determine the fact; none such have, as yet, been discovered or heard of. The cause of the deficiency of water evidently arises from the absence of very elevated chains fitted to preserve, under a burning climate, masses of snows sufficient to feed the rivers. The springs overflow on the heights and on the plains; but on the coast, sweet water must be drawn from a great depth. Those springs and wells which are found in the sandy deserts, furnish only bitter water impregnated with saltpetre. Chmate.] Arabia presents a variety of climates, according to the elevation of the surface, the nature of the soil, and the distance from the ocean; but in general its climate may be described as similar to that of N Africa. Snow sometimes falls in the interior, but seldom lies long. On the western coast the rains are periodical. In Yemen, they commence in the middle of June, and last till the middle of September. On the southern coast the rainy season begins in February, and ends in April; on the eastern coast it continues from the middle of November to the middle of February. In the northern districts, the rains regularly commence in December and January. It is at this season that the imagination of the traveller is most frequently deceived. The plains enamelled with flowers which salute him at every step,-the purity of the atmosphere, and the mildness of the temperature, - make him forget that the country which he then traverses is uninhabitable. But the scene quickly changes; the season of the rains ceases, and to a mild temperature succeeds a burning heat,— a sea of fire appears to blaze on every side, -and nothing meets the eye but a vast extent of arid soil covered with withered grass. In the driest tracts near the sea the dews are singularly copious. During the season of drought, the heat would be insupportable were it not tempered by a constant breeze: in places out of the direction of this breeze, the heat is incredibly intense. "Near Mecca," says Ali Bey, "one feels as if exposed in an actual furnace." In winter, the SW wind is insupportable. The skin is furrowed by it, and the pores are so closed that perspiration ceases. Only close and thick garments can ward off these effects. On the coasts of the Persian gulf, the SE wind is so humid, that, with a moderate heat, it provokes a troublesome sweat. The NW wind, more hot and dry, is less incommodious. During the three months of its prevalence, the air is more still; and when a hot breeze unexpectedly arises, it is capable of suffocating both men and animals. On the coast of Yemen the SE wind prevails for eight months, and is often so violent as to prevent all communication with the ports, even by vessels anchored in the roads.—The most dangerous wind is the simoom, sirocco, sam, or samiel. In the district between Bassora, Bagdad, Aleppo, and Mecca, it is most dreaded; and it blows only during the intense summer-heats. The Arabs of the desert, accustomed to an atmosphere of great purity, are said to perceive the simoom by its sulphurous odour before it reaches them. Its approach is also indicated by a redness in that part of the atmosphere whence it comes. On its approach, the Arabs throw themselves flat on the ground, and even the lower animals hold down their heads: those who are rash enough to face it are suddenly suffocated.—Many endemic maladies affect the inhabitants of A.: amongst others, the terrible elephantiasis, or leprosy, -a disease not so much owing to the climate as to the bad quality of the water and food. Ophthalmic diseases are common. At Mokka epidemic diseases prevail. The plague rarely penetrates into the country, the climate being generally wholesome; and many of the natives attain a great age without any disease. Inoculation is known and practised amongst the Arabs. Soil and Minerals.] The soil of A. is generally arid, and only fit for such plants as can support long drought, and are nourished with nocturnal dews. A portion of the hills in the interior, and on the seacoast, is solely composed of sands heaped together by the winds, which the first storm disperses. The soil of the maritime districts generally consists of clay mixed with sand, which is very productive when it can be sufficiently moistened. On the shore, however, are found entire districts covered with moving sands, and consequently wholly sterile. Near to Me dina volcanic traces have been observed, the soil being strewed with porous lava. There are reasons for believing that the interior of A. contains active volcanoes. In the island of Jebel-Tor, the traces of an extinguished volcano are very distinct.—The mountains of A. consist of porphyry, jasper, quartz, freestone, basalt, marble, alabaster, and limestone. Amongst its mineral productions are blue alabaster and several kinds of spar, agates, cornelians, tourmalins, the emerald, the onyx, gypsum, saltpetre, sulphur, naphtha, asphaltus, iron, lead, and copper. It does not appear to be very rich in metals; but the positive and unanimous testimony of the ancients will not permit us to doubt the former wealth of its mines. The country itself is vast, and there is no reason for doubting the truth of the statements which have been made with regard to its mineral wealth, any more than for doubting the ancient metallic wealth of Spain, as vouched by the Phœnicians, Carthaginians, and Romans, though little of that wealth is now to be found. In the mountains of Yemen, gold-mines were anciently wrought: sometimes the metal was found in the body of the rock, and sometimes in loose nodules on the surface. At present, however, there is no gold-mine known in A., nor any indication in the sand of its rivulets of so rich an intermixture. Iron, lead, and copper, however, are found in various places; and rock-salt is wrought near Loheia. Vegetable productions.] A great part of A., being destitute of water, is necessarily barren and burnt up, though a few plantschiefly of the saline and succulent kind-are found on the sandy plains, which serve to alleviate the thirst and afford a scanty meal to the camels in the painful journeys of the caravans. As to vegetation, the coasts are generally divested of it; but in the interior are numerous mountain-oases, shaded with date-trees and other species of palms. The sandy plains produce the same plants as N Africa. Amongst the plants of the interior, that of the indigenous coffee-shrub holds the first place; olives, sugar-canes, bananas, cocoa-trees, senna, manna, cotton, indigo, frankincense, dates, pomegranates, and various species of grapes, are cultivated. Forskäl, in the small extent of country which he examined, discovered several new genera; and Seetzen, had he lived to bring his treasures home, would no doubt have added greatly to the number. Among those worthy of notice, may be mentioned the Ricinus communis or castor-oil plant, and the senna shrub; also the Pandanus odoratissimus, the fragrance of which is celebrated by Arabian as well as Indian writers; and the Celastrus edulis, a tree cultivated by the Arabs in their coffee-plantations, the green leaves of which are supposed to be a preservative against the plague. The most valuable vegetable productions of A. are, the coffee-shrub, and the balm-tree or Amyris opobalsamum, which produces the balm of Mecca, the most fragrant and costly of all the gum-resins. The coffee grown at Bulgosa, near Beit-el-Fakeh, and exported from Mokka, is superior to any that has yet been imported into Europe from other quarters. Acacia vera, the tree from which gum-arabic is obtained, is frequently met with. The fruits of A. are, figs, pears, quinces, apricots, almonds, filberts, peaches, grapes, oranges, lemons, tamarinds, dates, and cocoa-nuts. The sugar-cane grows wild in Oman, and sugar is here manufactured in large quantities. Esculent vegetables,—such as melons, gourds, and all the cucurbitaceous tribe, with a variety of others less palatable to Europeans, are grown. The Arabs raise a great quantity of dhurra—Sorghum vulgare-a sort of coarse millet, which is extremely fructiferous, and which forms their chief article of food. They also cultivate wheat, barley, beans, rape, and lentils, but no oats. In feeding their horses and asses, they use barley and beans. Animal kingdom.] A. is the original country of the horse, the camel, and the wild ass. There is a race of oxen in A., with a hump on the back like those of Syria. Rock-goats inhabit the hills of A. Petræa; and the plains are stocked with gazelles. The sheep have broad thick tails, their wool is coarse, and their flesh by no means delicate. Hyænas, panthers, ounces, jackals, wolves, foxes, and wild-boars, exist in A.; and troops of monkeys inhabit the hills of Aden, and the southern forests of the peninsula. The jerboa, an animal of the opossum species, and the antelope, are constant inhabitants of the deserts. Amongst the animal productions of A., the horse and the camel hold deservedly the first rank. Of the former, the different breeds have multiplied almost beyond the power of enumeration,—every tribe having carefully preserved the genealogy of its horses and mares. All these breeds, however, may be reduced to five different sources, which have originated as many races. It is in the great deserts of Nejed that the best breeds are reared. So much is the Nejed breed valued, that a poor Arab at Antioch was known to refuse 36,000 plastres for a blood mare of that species. The Arabs give the preference to mares; and the dam, not the sire, determines the nobility of the individual animals with them. The power of enduring fatigue and hunger by these animals is astonishing. An emir, visited by the chevalier D'Arvieux, was saved of agriculture; and dikes and dams for collecting and I. by a mare, which carried him three days and nights without rest or food, and so conveyed him out of the reach of his enemies. Indeed, though those of the best blood are fleet, the Arabian horse is more distinguished for endurance than for speed; they are less remarkable for what they can do than for what they can suffer. Races sometimes take place among the Arabs of 40 to 50 m., in which the struggle is rather as to the power of bearing fatigue than of surpassing a rival in speed. The attachment of the Arab to his horse is almost as proverbial as the swiftness of the animal itself. The animal inhabits the same tent, is treated with as much care, and almost as much caressed as the children of the family; and this extraordinary domesticity gives the horse of the desert a tractableness and docility which no other breed possesses. The camel, however, is the most serviceable animal of A. It has been called 'the Ship of the Desert,' for without it the Arab could not cross the expanses of sand with which his country is covered. Its hoof is formed to tread on burning sands; it is patient of thirst; and the cartilaginous texture of its mouth enables it to feed upon the hard and prickly plants of the desert. Its milk is copious, and the flesh of its young tender; its dung supplies the deficiency of fuel, and its hair is manufactured into garments. In general the camel has a clean sleek coat, usually of a light brown colour, with a fringe of dark hair along the neck; but this covering, in the Arabian or Desert camel, is less profuse than in that of Upper Asia, where the climate requires a warmer coat. The eye of the camel is large, dark, soft, and prominent, resembling that of the gazelle; and it retains its brilliancy under the fiercest glare of the sun and sand. [Wellsted.] The Arabian camel is distinguished from the Bactrian species by having only one hump. The dromedary varies from the camel, not in species, but in breed; it is of a light and slender frame, and is used for running; the other, for bearing burthens. It is a commonly received notion that travelling Arabs, in their distress for want of water, are frequently reduced to slay their camels for the sake of finding a supply in the stomach; this expedient never came under the observation of Burckhardt, and he is inclined entirely to discredit it. Nothing like a supply of water could be found in the stomach of a camel, except on the same day on which it had been watered. Four complete days form the extreme length of time during which the Arabian camel can endure the absolute want of water in summer; the Darfur camels bear thirst much longer, often, it is said, to the extent of nine or ten days. But, it is to be observed, that, as in the case of several other ruminating animals, where a sufficiency of succulent herbage is obtained, the camel does not require water. A. has an excellent breed of asses, famous for speed and durability in travelling. In Yemen, the soldiers perform their patroles on asses, and every military service in which parade is not an object. A considerable number of these creatures are shipped from Oman to the eastern parts of Persia, and also to the isle of France. Buffaloes, oxen, and sheep with large and broad tails, are common; but the wool of the last is coarse, and the flesh by no means delicate. The oxen are nearly of the same breed as those common in India. There is too little water in A. for the buffalo. Among the wild animals, the hyæna is the most ferocious and terrible; the lions are neither so fierce nor so strong as those of Africa. Panthers and wild cats are found in various places. The wild apes—of which the country possesses a great variety—cause great damage to the coffee plantations.—Of birds, A. possesses different species of eagles, falcons, herons, and owls, and the ostrich. The partridge frequents the plains, the guinea-fowl the woods, and the pheasant the mountains. Aquatic fowls are rare. Fish abound on all the coasts; and on the SE coast is found the Pinna marina, or pearl-oyster, so famous in antiquity.—Reptiles are numerous: as tortoises, lizards, cameleons, serpents, vipers, and others. In some parts, a very large species of lizard called guaril is found, which the Arabians use for food. The whole line of sea-coast abounds in fish; and here the inhabitants live almost solely on fish, and in this respect correspond to the Ichthyophagi, or fish-eaters of antiquity. With the exception of the bee, all the insects of this region are noxious; a species of bird, which migrates from Eastern Persia, and destroys the destructive locusts, is almost adored by the natives. Agriculture.] Notwithstanding the sandy and ungrateful soil of the country, agriculture is farther advanced among the Arabians than in some other countries of the East. Their implements of husbandry, however, are sufficiently rude. The plough is simple in its construction; and is merely dragged over the field, in every direction, till the soil is thoroughly loosened for the reception of the seed. The sower follows the plough; and good crops are produced. In many parts of Yemen, the fields are cultivated like a garden; instead of a spade, they use a sort of hoe, and, in digging deep, a large crow, managed by two men, one of whom presses it into the ground, while the other draws it towards himself with cords. Niebuhr saw excellent wheat, Turkey corn, beans, and lentiles, in this district. The scarcity of water greatly increases and impedes the labours preserving the water are resorted to. In the plain of | Damar, the fields are watered out of deep draw-wells. The time of harvest varies greatly in different situations. At Muscat, wheat and barley are sown in December, and reaped in March. The ripe corn is torn up by the roots; but grass and green corn are cut with the sickle. Trade and Commerce.] Before the Portuguese discovered the cape of Good Hope, the Arabians monopolized the whole of the trade with India; but after the opening of that communication between the eastern and western worlds, the commerce of A. rapidly declined. Coffee is the staple article of commerce; and great quantities of it are exported from the ports of Mokka and Loheia. It was computed that 12,500,000 lbs. weight of this article was yearly exported from A. The European companies took off 1,500,000 lbs.; the Persians 3,500,000; the fleet from Suez 6,500,000; Hindostan, the Maldives, and the Arabian colonies on the coast of Africa, 50,000; and the caravans about 1,000,000. It is, however, understood, that Arabian coffee is being supplanted in many markets by that produced in the West Indies. The other exports of Arabia are dates, gum-arabic, myrrh, aloes, almonds, balm of Mecca, frankincense, some aromatic and medicinal drugs, and pearls. The pearl trade from the Persian gulf-which is in the hands of the Banians exclusively-amounts to 15 lacs of dollars annually. [Wellsted.] Imports consist chiefly of articles of ornament and apparel, weapons of defence, and corn. From Europe A. receives silver, iron, copper, lead, fire-arms, and gunpowder; from Abyssinia, slaves, sheep, elephants' teeth, and musk; from the eastern coast of Africa, gold, slaves, amber, and ivory; from Egypt, rice, lentiles, sugar, and oil; from Surat, linen; and from Coromandel, cotton. It may be here remarked, that the important article of cotton goods is first named in connexion with the early commerce of the Arabians, who in the 1st or 2d cent. of the Christian era brought them from India to the ports of the Red sea. [Baines.] The trade on the Red sea with Egypt has been almost annihilated by the long-enduring religious wars which have raged in Yemen and the Hejaz, and by the monopolies which have been created of the produce of A. The principal ports on the Arabian side of the Red sea are Jidda and Mokka. From Jidda the holy city of Mecca is principally supplied. But the imposts are very heavy. At Mokka the value of a fourth of the ship's cargo of coffee must be paid to the imam before it can be exported; and at Jidda, Europeans pay 8 per cent., and all other nations 13. The exports from Muscat are wheat, dates, horses, raisins, salt, dried fish, and drugs. The most valuable traffic of A. is still carried on by means of caravans; and pilgrims often mingle their devotion with commercial speculations. The yearly caravan of pilgrims from Cairo to Mecca, always creates a number of commercial transactions: a great proportion of those who visit the holy cities carry on some petty trade of exchange. [Bowring.] The A. merchant keeps no books, or at least what in Europe would be accounted such: he has a mere journal of sales and purchases; the annual operation of balancing his accounts, to form an estimate of his wealth, would be accounted impious. A merchant with a capital of £30,000, or £40,000, will often not even keep a clerk; he deals usually in one commodity; receives it from a correspondent in one town, and transmits it to one in another; and even if he carries on a retail trade, it is wholly for ready money. He has neither bills, nor bank-transactions, nor any mode whatever of creating a fictitious capital. The great centres of Arabian commerce are Jidda, Mokka, and Muscat. The bugulaks or merchant-ships of the Arabians are of singular construction. Their shape is that of a wedge, and their spars of great length. Their planks are merely nailed together, and not unfrequently sewn; and their whole frame-work is so crazy that they cannot be navigated during the SW monsoon in the Persian gulf, nor for more than seven months in the year along the coasts of A. and India. A. is wholly destitute of timber for ship-building, and these vessels are constructed on the Malabar coast. Aden, Makullah, and Shaher, employ about 70 vessels in the Indian trade, some of which are 200 tons. Population.] The population of the Arabian peninsula has been calculated at 12,000,000,-a small number for so extensive a region. But on this subject we can form nothing more than conjecture, having no data to enable us to arrive at a probable estimate. Some immense districts are entirely desert. The coasts are best-peopled; and perhaps the greater proportion of the inhabitants of A. are collected in the maritime districts. Arabs form, it is supposed, seven-eighths of the pop.; the remaining eighth consists of Jews, Banians, Turks, Negroes, Abyssinians, Franks, and others. The Jews are spread over the whole peninsula, and form, in some parts, colonies governed by their own sheikhs. The Banians, attracted from India by the profits of commerce, reside chiefly in the maritime towns. Like all Hindus, they are distinguished by the peculiarity of their habiliments, which are generally of a red colour. The Negroes are found in the cities, where they serve as slaves; in the south part of Nejed they form some distinct tribes. Arabs.] The native Arabs are divided into two classes: the agricultural and the nomadic,—the one a fixed race, with fixed dwellings,—the other a wandering race, living under tents, and moving in troops from place to place. Bedouins is the general and modern name of the latter, who are subdivided into a great variety of tribes. See article Bedouins. The Arabs are generally well-made, of middle stature, with meagre and pliant bodies; their colour is a blackish brown, or a yellow bordering on brown; their eyes are large, ardent, and black; their hair also is jet black; their beard strong and bushy. Fraser says: "Those of the superior order who came under our observation, as the sheikhs and their families, bore a strong characteristic resemblance to each other in features. The countenance was generally long and thin; the forehead moderately high, with a rounded protuberance near its top; the nose aquiline; the mouth and chin receding, giving to the line of the profile a circular rather than a straight character; the eye deep-set under the brow, dark and bright. Thin and spare, deficient in muscle, their limbs were small, particularly their hands, which were sometimes even of feminine delicacy. Their beards were almost always of a deep black, artificially coloured, if not naturally so; a few wore them grizzled." Baron Larrey considers the head of the Arab to be the most perfect type of that member, and to display the greatest physical perfection. Their women have generally attractive forms, lively eyes, a mouth and nose well-shaped, beautiful teeth, and a physiognomy full of expression. The Arab is furious and passionate; but if easily irritated, he is also easily appeased. His frankness, hospitality, love of liberty and independence, are the most marked traits of a character of which the principal defects have frequently no other source than in a very sanguine temperament and coarse education. The chief bad qualities of the Arab are his ardent thirst of revenge, his vanity, his superstition, his faith in talismans, and his inclination to robbery, but which he never practises upon his own tribe. He is the most refined and civilized of thieves; he pursues the traveller, robs him, and takes him prisoner; but at the same time uses him well, and never kills him. The Arab is very abstemious; his usual nourishment is a cake of dhourra, kneaded with camel's milk or oil; his ordinary drink is water; and sometimes he regales himself with coffee. The houses of the wealthier Arabs differ little from those of the Moors; they are plain stone-buildings, with flat roofs; the poor are content with mud huts, thatched with some kinds of herbs. In some places, the hut is formed of branches of trees covered with rush-mats, or with coarse cloth. The floors of the better class of houses are decorated with rich carpets; those of the poorer are covered with mats of straw. No man enters a house without putting off his shoes; and this is the more necessary, because chairs are as little known here as in Barbary. Cushions are spread round the walls; and as these seats are little elevated above the floor, to keep it clean is indispensably necessary. The common posture in which Arabs sit, is with their legs crossed under them: before their superiors, and at meals, they sit upon their heels with their knees touching each other. At meals, a large cloth is spread on the middle of the room. Upon this is set a small table about a foot in height, which supports a large round plate of tinned copper; and on this are placed the dishes which are likewise made of tinned copper. Each guest has a linen cloth or napkin upon his knees, which is necessary to wipe his fingers, as neither knives, forks, nor spoons are ever used. Boiled rice forms the principal part of the diet of the upper ranks. It is brought forward in a large wooden plate. Each eats in his turn; and after the principal guests are satisfied, the servants take their place, and finish what has been left. Carving is an operation seldom performed at an eastern table. The meat is divided into small pieces before it is brought forward; and if a piece be reckoned too large, it is torn with the fingers. This mode of eating, however, is not among Eastern nations, and particularly among Mahommedans, so uncleanly as it appears to us, or as it would in fact be in Europe. The Mahommedan religion enforces frequent ablutions upon its votaries; and no man sits down to table without having previously washed himself,-an operation which he is careful to repeat when he rises. The bread used by the poorer classes is made of a kind of millet called dhourra, and is prepared with grease, oil, butter, or camel's milk. In some places bread is made from barley; and the mode of preparing it is little different from that which prevails in some parts of the Highlands of Scotland. Besides rice, and the kind of bread already mentioned, the common articles of food are pulse, milk, and kiemak, or whipped cream. Mahomet forbade his followers the use of wine and spirituous liquors: but his precept has been but partially obeyed; many Arabians use spirituous liquors in private with much freedom; others procure a species of intoxication by smoking the dried leaves of a kind of hemp, called haschich. The smoking of tobacco is almost universal; and although opium is not chewed, as among the Turks, a substitute has been found for it by the Arabs in an herb called kaad. To conclude this general view of the habits and character of the Arabs, we extract the following from Burckhardt:- "The women cover their faces, as in Egypt, with a piece of cloth, in which there are two holes worked for the eyes, which are so large that half their face may be seen; and a few show nearly the whole. They all wear a sort of cloak, made of blue and white striped linen, as at Alexandria, which is put on with much grace; but, when a sight of their faces is obtained, the illusion is soon dispelled; for they are in general very ugly, with lemon-coloured complexions, like the men. Their faces and their hands, which are daubed all over with black, blue, and yellow, present a frightful picture to strangers; but custom has made them consider this painting as a sign of beauty. I saw some who had a ring passed through the cartilage of the nose, which hung down upon their upper lip. All the women I saw had a great deal of grace, and very fine eyes; but their hollow cheeks, painted of a greenish yellow, gave them the appearance of having the jaundice. Their noses are regular, but they have large mouths. They speak very well, and express themselves with great feeling. They engrave indelible drawings upon their skin, and stain their eyelids black, their teeth yellow, and their lips, feet, and hands, of a red tile colour, like the Egyptians, and with the same materials. Their dress consists of an immense pantaloon, that descends into their slippers, or half boots, of yellow leather, and is composed of Indian striped cotton. The poorer sort wear them of blue cloth. They have, besides, a shift of a size and form the most extravagant. It is composed of two square cloths, six feet long and five broad, which are united at the upper part, except an opening in the middle, to pass the head through. The lower corners are cut out about seven inches, like the segment of a circle; so that what was before an angle becomes a hollow slope. These slopes are both sewed; but the lower part and the sides remain open from top to bottom. The rich wear these shifts made of slight, striped silk tissue, as fine as gauze, which comes from Egypt, and gather them in plaits on each side upon the shoulders, binding them round the waist with a belt. Above these they wear a caftan of India cotton. I never saw them wear any other ornament upon the head than a handkerchief; but they put rings and bracelets upon their hands, arms, legs, and feet, like the women in other Mussulman countries. The Bedouin women, or those that live in the interior of the country, and appear to be of the highest rank, have for their only costume a large shift of blue stuff; a cloth of a coquelicot colour upon their faces; a very large cloak, or black veil of wool; some rings, bracelets, and a few other jewels. The costume of the men at Mekka is, as in Egypt, composed of a benish, or exterior caftan, bound with a belt, a shirt, drawers, and babouches, or slippers; but this is the dress of persons in place, merchants, and those employed about the temple, &c. The lower people have hardly ever more than a shirt and drawers. The Bedouin Arab wears commonly a large cloak without sleeves over his tunic, made of a tissue of coarse wool, or of a slight cloth, both sides of which are alike, and commonly with alternate stripes of brown and white, each a foot broad. The inhabitants of the city wear red caps and turbans; but the Bedouins do not: they cover their heads with a handkerchief, striped yellow, red, and black, folded diagonally in the form of a triangle, and simply thrown upon the head; so that two of the angular points fall before the shoulders, and the other behind the neck upon the back. Those that are rich wear a piece of muslin twisted round the head, above the handkerchief, in the form of a turban; but the poor go almost naked. With the exception of those employed about the temple, and a small number of merchants, the people go always armed. The arms that are most common are: the large curved knife, halbert, lance, mace, and some few guns. The knives have sheaths of a most singular form; for, independent of the space occupied by the blade, it is prolonged about a foot, in a semicircular form, and terminated by a ball, or some other ornament, more or less carved. It is hung obliquely before the body, the handle towards the left side, with the point upwards; so that the movement of the right arm is greatly impeded by this position, which is maintained by force of custom. So true is it, that men of all ranks and countries are subject to the caprices of fashion. Some Arabs carry axes, nearly two feet long; and others go armed with a stick, five feet long and two inches thick, with an iron point at the bottom of it. The horsemen carry a lance ten feet and a half long, ornamented with a tuft of black feathers at the jointing of iron, the other end being also armed with a small point, which the bearer sticks perpendicularly in the ground when he alights. I saw some Arabs of Yemen armed with a sword and shield: the former was straight and broad; the latter of metal, hardwood, or the skin of the hippopotamus, (those of the latter substance were the best,) and all were ornamented with carvings. They were about a foot broad. The people of the country assured me, that the ceremonies of marriages and births were not accompanied by any feasts or rejoicings, as in other countries; and I myself did not see any celebrated. Interments take place without any ceremony. They carry the body to the foot of the Kaaba, where the faithful who are present repeat a short prayer for the deceased after the ordinary canonical prayer; and they carry away the corpse to bury it in a ditch outside the town. There are a number of hand-barrows for this purpose before one of the doors of the temple, in the public path; one of which is engaged by the family of the deceased, who place the body upon it, dressed in its ordinary habit, without the least ornament, or even the covering of a pall. After the interment, they bring the barrow to the place from whence they took it. I remarked, that in all Arabia, it is customary to make three perpendicular incisions upon each cheek; in consequence of which, the greater part of the men are adorned with this fine mark, that is to say, six large scars. Having inquired of many persons the object of this custom, I was informed by some, that it was to make themselves bleed, and by others, that it was a mark by which they declared themselves slaves to the house of God. But the truth is, that it is fashion which recommends this sacrifice; and they look upon it as a beauty, equal to the blue, red, and black paintings, or the nose-rings of the women, or their own knives, which impede all their movements." A. contains many small states, of which those of the imams of Yemen and Muscat, and the sherrif of Mecca are the chief, and a countless horde of independent tribes. Each nomadic tribe has its own emir or sheik, chosen from amongst the class of nobles, whom it obeys in time of war, and who manages all the internal administration, but in conjunction with the elders of the tribe. The Koran forms the sole legal code of the country; and where it is silent, all is left to the decision of the judge. Amongst the Bedouins, the laws are ordinarily transmitted from mouth to mouth, or from one to another; their jurisprudence is simple in the extreme; advocates are unknown, and all is set forth viva voce, and decided in the camp. Their usual punishments are loss of the beard, which is regarded as an infamy, and bastinading the soles of the feet. Those condemned to death are hanged, beheaded, empaled, or burned, according to the magnitude of the crime, or in some cases the ferocity of the judge. Language.] The Arabic language is divided into two dialects: that of the Koran, or the literate, and the vulgar Arabic. The latter is subdivided into dialects more or less corrupted; the purest is that of Sanaa in Yemen. The Arabic spoken in the present day is said to differ as much from the Arabic of the Koran as the Italian from the Latin; but the welleducated Arabs, like the Greeks of the Lower empire, still use it in their correspondence and literary composition. The number and variety of its grammatical forms is great; but its strong gutturals and deep intonation render it harsh to the ears of a European. In harmony and energy the Arabic is said to excel; and its richness with respect to words exceeds all other languages. The Arabs, indeed, represent it as so copious that no uninspired man can be a perfect master of it; as instances of its luxuriance, they allege that it has a thousand terms to express sword, five hundred for lion, and two hundred for serpent. In consequence of being the language of Mahommed, the Arabic has been diffused over a larger portion of the earth than any other. It is studied, and understood, if not spoken, from the shores of the Atlantic to the banks of the Ganges; and from the steppes on the Volga to the countries on the Niger. In the Sunda, Molucca, and Philippine isles, it is generally spoken. Throughout the greater part of Spain also, and the whole of Sicily, together with the eastern coast of Africa as far as Madagascar, it has been prevalent. This vast extension may be one great cause of its copionsness, as it doubtless is of its diversity of dialects and pronunciation. The language of one province is almost unintelligible to the inhabitants of another. The dialect of the highlanders of Yemen, from their little intercourse with strangers, bears the strongest resemblance to the language of the Koran. The Ismaelitic forms of the language prevail in the N of A.; the Himyaritic, in the S; and the Koreish or Koraitic in Mecca and the surrounding country. We have valuable grammars of the Arabic language by Erpen, Michaelis, Richardson, Jahn, Rosenmuller, De Sacy, Tychsen, Ewald, and Roorda; and good lexicons by Giggeius, Golius, Meninski, Richardson, Abulfuyl, and Freytag. The new or vulgar Arabic has been investigated by Canes, Savary, Eichhorn, Burckhardt, and others; and of the Algerine Arabic several grammars and vocabularies have recently appeared in Paris. The idiom of the Arabs in the NW part of Africa is the most corrupt of any except that of Malta. Religion and Literature.] Islamism, born in Arabia, is still the prevalent religion. Amongst other religious sects-which abound here-are the Zeidites, the Abadites, and the Messechalites, and since the middle of the 18th cent., the Wahabites. The Arabs were once celebrated for their literature. The variety, elevation, and originality of their poetry, the richness of their historical collections, and the extent of their knowledge, are still the theme of admiration; but the golden epoch of Arabian learning was several ages anterior to the time of Mohammed. The disorders which followed the destruction of the khalifate, - the irruptions of the Turks,—and to crown all, the isolation of the peninsula-for the Othmans deprived them of all communication with the civilized world—seem to have conspired to plunge into a state of profound lethargy, a lively, an ingenious, and intelligent people. A., however, is still, in respect of mental culture, superior to Turkey; and its language has remained, as the language of the saints and the sciences, that of the far greater part of the Mussulmans; but the positive knowledge of the Arabs has much decayed; their astronomy is only astrology; their mathematics, a coarse species of surveying; and all their philosophy is founded on the Koran. Their great historians and geographers, their logicians who were formed in the school of Plato and Aristotle, have all disappeared; and their medical science has degenerated into empiricism. History.] The authentic history of A. scarcely ascends to the 5th cent. of our era. According to the Oriental historians—upon whom, however, little dependence can be placed—the Arabians are sprung from Kahtan, or Joktan, the fifth in descent from Shem, and from Adnan, descended in a direct line from Ishmael, the son of Abraham. The posterity of the former are called genuine or pure Arabs; those of the latter, naturalized or insititious Arabs, from having settled in A. many centuries after the Joktanides had possession of the country. Yarab, one of the sons of Joktan, is stated to have founded the kingdom of Yemen; and Jorham, another son, that of Hedjaz. Saba, the fourth king of Yemen, is said to have built a stupendous reservoir for collecting the water which descended from the mountains, which broke down during the reign of one of his descendants, and drowned a vast number of the inhabitants. Owing to the destruction caused by this inundation, eight tribes were compelled to abandon their homes, and seek for other settlements. Some removed into Chaldea, and founded the kingdom of Hira; others settled in Syria-Damascena, and gave rise to the kingdom of Ghassan. Himyar, the immediate successor of Saba, gave his name to the kingdom of Himyar, and his descendants were called Himyarites, the Homerites of Ptolemy and other Greek writers. He was succeeded by a long line of hereditary princes. The last, save one, was Yusof, surnamed Dsu-Nowaus, who lived about 70 years before Mahomet. He was a Jew, and a fierce persecutor of the Christians. The patriarch of Alexandria having instigated the negus or king of Abyssinia to avenge the Christian cause, the forces of Dsu-Nowaus were completely routed, and he himself, pursued by the Abyssinians, spurred his horse into the sea and perished. In 549, Abrahah, an Abyssinian general, assumed the government of Himyar and Yemen, and the Christian religion was established in Yemen. Seyff, or Seif-Ben-Dsi-Yezen, of the royal family of Himyar, having procured the assistance of the king of Persia, succeeded in expelling the Abyssinians from Yemen, and seating himself on the throne of his ancestors. His reign, however, was of short duration; and with him terminated the race of Himyar. On his death the government of Yemen devolved on the lieutenants of the Persian monarch, who bore the title of ameers, or emirs. The posterity of Jorham are represented to have reigned in Hedjaz till the time of Ishmael, who married into this tribe. His second son, Kedar, succeeded to his power. Of the successors of Kedar nothing is known; but it would appear that, about the time of Alexander the Great, Hejaz was divided among several independent tribes, whose jarring interests occasioned a continual warfare. Of these, the most powerful were the Koreish and the Khozaites: the former were of the posterity of Ishmael, and were considered the noblest of the Arabian tribes. The Khozaites were a colony from Yemen, who had emigrated on the breaking down of the reservoir there, and, establishing themselves at Mecca, had usurped the government of that place, till Kosa, of the tribe of Koreish, wrested it from them. Among the descendants of Kosa, Abd Menof is celebrated for his piety, and Amru, surnamed Hashem, for his wisdom and generosity. The memory of the latter is held in such veneration that the posterity of Mahomet still call themselves Hashemites; and the princes of Mecca to this day take the title of El Imam El Hashem, or 'the prince of the Hashemites.' Abdul Motalleb, the son of Hashem, was the grandfather of Mahomet; and it was not one of the least recommendations of that extraordinary man that he was thus nobly related. In the language of his countrymen, he sprung from the race of Thenanah, the best of the Arabs; from the tribe of Koreish, the best of Thenanah; and was the direct descendant of Hashem, the best of the Koreish. Mahomet, or Mohammed, the only son of Abdallah and Ameinah, was born at Mecca on the 10th of November, 570. Left an orphan before his eighth year, he was consigned to the care of his uncle, Abu Taleb, the pontifical head of the tribe, who carried on an extensive trade with the neighbouring tribes. By this guardian he was instructed in the arts of war and of merchandise; he accompanied him to the fairs of Syria, and fought with him in some conflicts with the predatory Arabian tribes. When in his twenty-fifth year, being recommended as factor to Khadija, the widow of a wealthy trader, he conducted himself so much to her satisfaction that she made him her husband. He was now enabled to live as became the nephew of the protector of the kaaba, and to vie in splendour with the richest in Mecca. Nothing further is recorded of him till he reached the fortieth year of his age, when he began to disclose his pretended mission. This period of Mahomet's life corresponded with that of the greatest depression of the Persian and Roman empires, so that nothing was to be feared from the intervention of those once formidable powers. Heresies had also about this time greatly increased in the Christian church; and abstruse dogmas and idolatrous adoration were generally substituted for the purity and simplicity of the gospel. The abuses and corruptions which at this time so grossly pervaded every Christian sect, and the endless religious controversies and contentions which convalsed the Eastern world, might first suggest to Mahomet the idea of a reformation, and the general incorporation of all sects in one great faith. To conciliate all parties, and make his opinions acceptable to every description of religionists, he assumed, as the foundation of his system, some of those points concerning which most were agreed; while, in his other doctrines and institutions, he skilfully addressed himself to the habits, the passions, and prejudices, of his countrymen. The success of his imposture is known to all. The conquest of Mecca was the signal for the submission of the rest of A.; and ambassadors poured in upon the prophet from all quarters, to make submission in the name of their different tribes. Between the taking of Mecca and the time of his death, not more than three years elapsed; but in this short period he had destroyed the idols of Arabia; had extended his conquests to the borders of the Greek and Persian empires; and had tried his arms against the disciplined troops of the former, and defeated them in a desperate encounter at Muta. His throne was now firmly established, and an impetus given to his followers which in a few years enabled them to subdue a great portion of the globe. Part of India, Persia, the Greek empire, the whole of Asia Minor, Egypt, Barbary and Spain, were successively reduced by their arms; and although Mahomet did not live to see such mighty conquests, he laid the first foundations of this wide-spreading dominion. He died at Medina on the 8th of June, 632. On the death of Mahomet, Abu-Bekr was elected khalif or successor of Mahomet in the spiritual as well as temporal empire which he had erected. The fall of Bossra, which was hastened by the treachery of the Roman governor, opened the way to that of Damascus. The battle of Aiznadin, in which 50,000 Christians are said to have been slain, decided the fate of the capital of Syria in 633. Emesa and Baalbek were taken the following year; and the Syro- Grecians made a last and ineffectual stand in the open field, on the banks of the Hieromax. Jerusalem sustained a siege of four months, at the end of which period the patriarch Sophronius delivered up the holy city to Omar in person, who had succeeded to the khalifate on the death of Abu-Bekr. The conquest of Aleppo. in 638, after a long siege, and that of Antioch which followed. completed the subjugation of Syria. The fall of Alexandria before the forces of Amrou decided the fate of Egypt in the same year. The battle of Kadesia, and the capture of Medayen (Ctesiphon) had already made the Moslems the masters of Persia almost to the banks of the Oxus. But while his arms were subjugating the finest provinces of the East, Omar fell by the hand of an assassin; and Othman, the secretary of Mahomet, was proclaimed khalif. Othman's administration was feeble, and failed to curb the Moslem chiefs now elated with power and flushed with victory. An insurrection broke out against him, which was fomented by Ayesha, the widow of the prophet; and Othman was besieged and slain in his palace in the 82d year of his age, and the 35th of the Hegira. All, who had married Fatima, the daughter of Khadija, and the only surviving child of the prophet, was now invited to the throne of A. But his reign was short and tumultuous. Moawiyah, the son of Abu-Sofian, possessed the affections of the army of Syria, and the various lieutenants throughout the empire refused to recognise the authority of Ali. Two powerful chieftains, Telha and Zobeir, escaped into Irak, accompanied by Ayesha, who bore an implacable hatred towards the husband and family of Fatima, and there raised the standard of revolt. Ali marched at the head of his followers from Medina to Bassora, where he encountered and defeated the rebels. Telha and Zobeir were both slain, and Ayesha was led a captive into the tent of Ali, who dismissed her to her proper station at the tomb of her husband. Ali then marched his victorious troops against Moawiyah, who had assumed the title of khalif. The rival khalifs met on the plains of Seffein; and during several months various battles took place with various success. A negotiation ensued: but three Charegites-a sect of religious and political zealotsconsidering that nothing but the deaths of Ali, Moawiyah, and Amrou, could restore tranquillity to A., entered into a confederacy to destroy them. They poisoned their swords, and each chose his victim. Moawiyah was wounded, but recovered; Amrou's secretary received the blow which was meditated for his master; and Ali was mortally stabbed in the mosque at Kufah. He died in the 63d year of his age, and left behind him the character of the bravest and most virtuous of the Mahommedan khalifs. His son Hasan succeeded to the khalifate, but soon resigned in favour of Moawiyah, and retired to Medina, where he was poisoned at the instigation of the latter. Moawiyah, now supreme lord of the Moslem world, transferred the seat of empire to Damascus, and sent a powerful army, under his son Yezid, to besiege Constantinople, the seat of the Roman empire; but the army returned to Syria without performing any service of importance. The Moslem arms, however, were more successful in Tartary and Africa, under Saad and Okbha. On the death of Moawiyah, and the accession of Yezid, an attempt was made to reinstate the family of Ali on the throne; but it proved unsuccessful. Yezid, however, behaved with elemency to the rival race; and the reputed descendants of Ali and Fatima are still numerous throughout the Mahommedan world. In Arabia, Persia, and India, they are styled shereefs, or seids; in Syria and Turkey, emirs. From a. D. 661 to 750, the house of Moawiyah, commonly called the dynasty of the Ommiades, continued to enjoy the khalifate; but in the reign of Merwan, an insurrection was raised in favour of the great-grandson of Abbas, the uncle of the prophet, which terminated in a general massacre of the descendants of Moawiyah. The first of the Abassides now fixed his court at Kufah, whence it was transferred to Haschemiah on the Euphrates; and Al- Mansor, the second prince of the family, erected the magnificent city of Bagdad, which continued to be the residence of the Mahommedan khalif till its destruction by Hulaku the Tartar, about the middle of the 13th cent. But the civil jurisdiction of the khalifate gradually diminished in the extent and power of its sway. Real or nominal descendants of Ali and Fatima had possessed themselves of the thrones of Egypt and Western Africa; and a prince of the Ommiades, who escaped the general massacre of his family, was founder of the caliphat of Cordova in Spain. Thus the sovereignty of A. was lost by the extent and rapidity of foreign conquest; and from being the seat and centre, it sank into a mere province of the Mahommedan empire. About the beginning of the 16th cent., the Turks took captive the last of the Abassides, and received from him, at Constantinople, the formal renunciation of the khalifate. The keys of the temple of Mecca were also delivered up by the Fatimite shereef; and from this time the ecclesiastical supremacy has belonged to the Turkish sultans. The remaining history of A., with the exception of what relates to two sects of religionists, the Karmathians and the Wahabys, exhibits nothing more interesting than the squabbles of petty chieftains, and the rise and fall of different sheikhdoms or principalities. Near the close of the 9th cent, (the 277th year of the Hegira,) a prophet named Karmath appeared in the neighbourhood of Kufah, and soon gained an immense number of followers, occasioning great disturbance throughout A. Persecution assisted the progress of this new sect, which aimed at an entire reformation of Mahommedanism. The Karmathians made themselves masters of Bahrein; Bassorah and Kufah were successively taken and pillaged; and Abu-Thaher, the successor of Karmath, led his troops across the Desert to the holy city, where 30,000 citizens were put to the sword.
