The black stone of the Kaaba was borne away in triumph by the conqueror, but was afterwards restored. With a considerable principality in the heart of A., these Karmathians continued, for a long time, the scourge of the khalifate, obliging it to pay them an annual tribute that the pilgrimage to Mecca might be allowed to be regularly performed. Another formidable sect, the Wahabys, more than half a century ago started up in the province of Nejed. The efforts of this party were originally directed solely to religious reform, or an attempt to bring back Mahommedanism to its early simplicity. Abdel Wahabe, the founder of this sect, was a native of El-Howta, the chief seat of a tribe of the name of Temyn, in Nejed. He was a man of education, having pursued his studies successively at Bassorah, Bagdad, and Damascus. His first doctrines probably extended no further than to his own peculiar interpretation of the Koran; and his disciples were confined, for several years, to a few tribes of the desert. By degrees, however, they spread more widely; and the design of reforming the religion of his country seems to have given place in his mind to that of establishing a new one. Different accounts are given of his creed, but it seems, in substance, to approach nearly to pure theism. The Atubis, the most powerful of the tribes who inhabit the coast, adopted the tenets of the Wahabys. The holy shrine at Carbela was attacked by them in 1802, the tombs destroyed, and the town ransacked. The Wahabys now aspired to the possession of Mecca and Medina. Ghalib, the reigning sherif, was so unpopular among his subjects, that even his brother-in-law, Mozeifé, deserted to the Wahabys. Abdul-Aziz entrusted Mozeifé with the command of 12,000 men, and, in several battles, he defeated the sherif; and on the 27th of April, 1803, the holy city fell into the hands of the Wahaby general. In 1804, Medina, with all its riches, fell into the hands of the Wahabys, and the tomb of the prophet was destroyed. Jidda also was attacked, but without success. In 1807, Saoud, the successor of Abdul-Aziz, issued orders that every Turk should leave A .- an order which extended to the Turkish pasha, and the kadis of Mecca, Medina, and other places. The authority of the sherif was now annihilated, and the judicial power fell into the hands of the Wahabys. In 1814, Saoud died, and was succeeded by his son Abdullah. In 1815, Mecca was taken by Mohammed Ali, pasha of Egypt, and the Wahabys were compelled to evacuate the territories of the Hedjaz. They retreated to Deraiyeh, where Abdullah was besieged by Ibrahim Pasha, to whom he surrendered in 1818. But the power of this party, though broken, is by no means annihilated; and they have of late years offered considerable resistance to the Egyptian authority on the shores of the Persian gulf. Authorities. Niebuhr's Travels. - Valentia's Travels. -Travels of Ali Bey.-Burckhardt's Travels in Arabia; and Notes on the Bedouins.—Wellsted's Travels. ARABISTA'N, [i. e., 'Land of the Arabs,'] a vast extent of level country in the Persian prov. of Khúzistán, comprising the districts of Hawiyah, Shushter, and Dizful.
ARABKIR. See ARABGIR.
ARABO. See RAAB.

ARABOG, or Rabogh, a small sea-port town in Arabia, on the coast of the Red sea, in N lat. 22° 31', E long. 38° 52'. Those pilgrims to Mecca who arrive from beyond sea, here commence their religious rites and ablutions.

ARACAN, or ARRACAN, a British prov. of Hindostan, on the E coast of the bay of Bengal, forming the western part of the peninsula beyond the Ganges. It extends from N lat. 20° 46' to about 16°; and between E long. 92° and 95°. The length of the prov. is 250 m.; its greatest breadth is 105 m.; its average breadth is 50 m.; but in some parts it is little more than 10 m. On the N it is separated from the Chittagong district by the river Nauf. A range of mountains called Yeomandong, running parallel to and in some places approaching very near the coast, separates it from the Birmese empire. The summits of this chain vary in height from 2,000 to 8,000 ft. The principal rivers are the Aracan, the Nauf, the Aeng, and the Sandoway. No fewer than 22 passes exist in this chain, between A. and Ava, of which the principal, the pass of Aeng, has an alt. at its summit of 4,664 ft. These passes are generally little more than narrow footpaths, or fit only for being traversed by ponies and bullocks; but that of Aeng is a good road, constructed with considerable skill by the Birmese, since 1816. For a distance of 6 m. from Aeng it is quite level, and about 20 ft. wide; from Aeng it runs to Sarowah, on the Aeng river, a distance of 15 m.; from Sarowah to Nairiengan on the summit of the dividing range, whence it descends into the bed of the Mine river, and in the district of Lehdine reaches the plains of Ava. The sea-coast is fronted by numerous islands, the largest of which are Cheduba and Ramree. The country, though fertile, has as yet been very imperfectly cultivated, and is indeed almost en- 16,500 sq. m., only 400 were under cultivation. The climate is extremely unhealthy, from the immense quantity of rain which falls throughout the year. At the town of A. 15 inches of rain have been known to fall in one day! And in the two months of July and August alone, the total quantity has amounted to 103 inches. The SW monsoon begins early in May, and lasts till the end of October. The export trade-which is carried on chiefly with Bengal and Chittagongconsists in ivory, wax, gold, silver, rice, salt, timber, and young horses. The export of rice has greatly increased of late years. The shipments of that article in 1845-6 exceeded 12 lacs of rupees, or £120,000, and extended to China, Bourbon, and the markets of Europe. The present revenue is about three lacs and a half of rupees, or £350,000, produced principally by the rental of land, the East India company considering themselves the proprietors of the soil. The pop., at one period, is understood to have amounted to upwards of 2,000,000; but in 1831 it was estimated so low as 173,928. This great reduction is said to have been produced by the tyrannical conduct of the Birmese government. The inhabitants, chiefly Mughs, are a hardy inoffensive race, professing the religion of Buddha. They are well-educated by the priests, so that nearly all can read and write.—The history of this country, according to native annals, begins in A. D. 701; and continues through a series of 120 native princes to 1783. According to these annals, the kingdom at different times comprehended Ava, and extended over part of China on the one hand, and part of Bengal on the other. In 1783, this country was conquered, after a feeble resistance, by the Birmese. The Mughs subsequently made various efforts to regain their independence, more especially in 1810, under a native chief called King Berring, who succeeded for a time in subjecting the whole of the country to his authority. The Birmese, however, reconquered the country, and in pursuit of the fugitives committed numerous aggressions on the frontiers of Chittagong. At last growing bold by what they supposed to be the timidity of the British government, they took possession, in 1824, of the island of Shapuri on the Chittagong side of the Nauf river. On the 5th of March, in that year, war was declared against the Birmese; and in the beginning of the following year, Colonel Morrison invaded A. from Chittagong, and, after a long and fatiguing march of 150 m., took possession of the capital, with a trifling loss, on the 1st of April. By the treaty of Yandabu, Feb. 24th, 1826, the Birmese ceded this district with other territories to the English. The town of Aracan, the cap. of the above prov., is about 40 m. from the sea, in N lat. 20° 35', E long. 93° 15', on a small plain nearly encircled by an irregular mass of hills, none of which exceed 500 ft. in height. It is intersected by a branch of the Aracan river, which is navigable for boats at high water. The banks of this river are low, and on the reflux of the tide—the average rise of which is about 8 ft.—every species of mud and filth is deposited on them; yet in such situations most of the inhabitants reside in houses raised upon piles. The hills by which the town is surrounded are isolated heights, and the space between them is occupied by streams, morasses, and shallow lakes. Nearly all the surface in the direction of the hills is covered with wood; below the town, the river breaks into a number of branches, which so thickly intersect all the country between the town and the sea, that a space of 200 sq. m. is nothing but a mass of dense vegetation, mud, and marsh. The pop. probably amounts to 10,000.

ARACARI, a river of South America, which falls into the Rio Negro near a town of the same name. ARA tirely covered with thick jungle. In 1825, out of | -Also an island on the coast of Brazil, in S lat. 26° 20'.
ARACATI, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Seara, near the mouth of the river Jaguaribe. Pop. 600.
ARAÇATIBA, a village and large forest-district of Brazil, in the prov. of Espiritu Santo, on the river Jucu.
ARACENA, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the Sierra Morena, 30 m. NW of Seville. Pop. 2,100.

ARACHIEH, a lake in the oasis of Siwah, 25 leagues W from Siwah. Drovetti describes it as from 6 to 7 leagues in circumf. It contains an island, consisting entirely of naked rocks, but which is held in great veneration by the natives.
ARACHOVA. ARAKHOVA.
ARACLEA. See EREKLI.

ARACTHUS, a river of Albania, rising in the Greater Metzovo portion of the chain of Pindus, and flowing SW into the gulf of Arta. Its valley is separated by a calcareous ridge from the plains of Joannina.

ARAD, one of the Bahrein islands, in the Persian gulf. It lies nearly N and S, and is low and sandy, with a narrow isthmus dividing it into two parts. The channel between it and Bahrein island is about a mile in breadth. See BAHREIN ISLANDS.

ARAD-VARMEGYE, a county or administrative district of Hungary, bounded on the S by the river Marosch; on the E by Transylvania. Superficies 2,700 sq. m. The plains produce wheat, maize, saffron, and tobacco. The hills are covered with vineyards, and great numbers of cattle are reared on the pastures. Of 850,828 jochs of cultivable land, about 400,000 are under cultivation. Its population, composed chiefly of Wallachians, but including Hungarians, Germans, Servians, and Armenians, amounted in 1833 to 229,487. It is divided into four jaras: viz., Arad, Siri, Sarand, and Boros-Jeno; and comprises 1 town, 22 burghs, and 160 villages. ARAD (OLD), [Hung. Urad-O,] the cap. of the above district, is situated on the r. bank of the Marosch, 25 m. N of Temeswar, in N lat. 46° 9', E long. 21° 17'. It is irregularly built; but contains some good houses, a college, gymnasium, and other public establishments. Near it are the ruins of the castle of Arad. The chief cattle-market in the kingdom is held here; and its fairs are next in importance to those of Pest and Debreczin. Pop. in 1827, 13,824. ARAD (NEW), [Hung. Arad-Uj,] a town, or fortress rather, about 3 m. from the above, in the Banat, on the opposite side of the Marosch. It commands the passage of the river, on the high road between Temeswar and Grosswardein. A few scattered barracks and magazines, and a church, form the principal edifices: all the rest is park and garden, with parterres and alleys, girt by high ramparts, and a great circle of bomb-proof casemates.
ARADEO, a town of Naples, in the Terra d'Otranto, 13 m. NW of Gallipoli. Pop. 1,254.

ARADI, a town of Anadoli, 15 m. NW of Kastamuni, near a stream of the same name. ARAFAT, [ARAB. Jebel-el-Orfat,] a hill in Arabia, 15 m. SE from Mecca. It is a granitic rock about 200 ft. high, situated at the foot of a higher hill to the ESE, in a plain about 2 m. across. On the E side, broad stone steps lead up to the top; and a broad unpaved path, on the W, over rude masses of granite with which the declivity is covered. The summit commands a very extensive and singular prospect. Towards the western extremity of the plain are seen Bir-Bazan and the Aalamein; somewhat nearer, southwards, the mosque called Djama-Nimre, or Djama-Seydna- Ibrahim; and on the SE a small house where the shereef used to lodge during the pilgrimage. On the eastern side of the mountain, and close to its foot, are the ruins of a small mosque built on rocky ground, called Djama-el-Szakhrat, where Mahomet was accustomed to pray, and where the pilgrims make four prostrations in memory of the prophet. Several large reservoirs lined with stone are dispersed over the plain; two or three of these are close to the foot of Arafat. They are filled from the same aqueduct which supplies Mecca, and the head of which is about 11 hour distant, in the eastern mountains. The canal is left open here for the convenience of pilgrims, and is conducted round the three sides of the mountain. The Mahommedans say that here Adam first met Eve, after they had been expelled from Paradise, and separated 120 years! It is usually visited by the pilgrims to Mecca. In 1807 there were assembled here, 80,000 men, 2,000 women, and 1,000 children, in whose service were 70,000 camels, horses, and asses. Of these there were 45,800 Wahabees mounted. The indefatigable Burckhardt visited this place in July 1814. He estimated the number of devotees then present at 70,000. In this Babel, he reckoned about forty languages; and he had no doubt there were many more. A sermon delivered on the mount constitutes the main ceremony of the Hadj. or Hhaj, and entitles the hearer to the name and privileges of a Hadji, or Hhaji. After concluding their ceremonies here, the pilgrims set out for Mecca, passing through the valley of Mina, in which they spend some time in "stoning the devil." This ceremony consists in throwing stones against small pillars set up at each end of the valley. Several thousand sheep and goats are then sacrificed. The third day brings them back to Mecca, where some further ceremonies finish the festival.-Pitts.-Burckhardt.-Ali Bey.
ARAFAXA, a town of Sweden, in West Bothnia, on the Tornea, 32 m. N of the town of Tornea.
ARAFURAS. See ALFORAS.

A'RAGON, or ARRAGON, commonly called the kingdom of Aragon, a prov. of Spain, bounded on the N by the Pyrenees, by which it is separated from France; on the E by Catalonia; on the SE and S by Valencia; on the SW by New Castile; and on the W by Old Castile and Navarre. It lies between the parallels of 40° and 43° N. Its greatest length from N to S is 200 m.; and its average breadth about 130 m. It has an area of about 15,400 sq. m. The portion of the Pyrenean range which belongs to A. forms the loftiest in the chain, rising at its central point, in the summit of Monte Perdido, or Mont Perdu, to the height of 11,168 ft. above sea-level; and interpenetrates with its ramifications the entire northern section of the prov., forming the rich and beautiful vallies of Venasque, Gistan, Tena, Canfranc, Aragues, and Ansó, which descend with a rapid slope to the basin of the Ebro. The SW confines are skirted by the lofty ranges of Moncayo, Cuenco, Molina, and Albarracin; and the SE by the Sierra Morella. Running towards the S is the Sierra Teruel, which, at the city of that name, attains an elevation of 4,331 ft.; and extending in a NW direction, is the range of the Morata-del-Conde, the culminating point of which, between Almunia and Frasno, is Monte Cayo or Moncayo, alt. 4,921 ft. The central portion of A. consists of the basin of the Ebro, which runs from SE to NW, and receives on the l. the Arvo, the Gallego, and the united streams of the Cinca and Segre; and on the r. the Xiloca or Xalon-which rises in the celebrated fountain of Cella, in the S Albarracin, at an elevation of 3,700 ft. above sea-level—the Guerra, the Aguas or Almonaced, the San Martin, the Guadaloupe, and the Nonaspe, which, with its tributary the Alguas, forms a partial line of boundary between A. and Catalonia.—The soil and climate of A. correspond in character to the variations in its elevation and aspect. In the mountainous districts, especially those of the Pyrenees, snow frequently lies to the depth of 4 or 5 ft. so late as the month of June; while in the adjacent vallies and plains the temperature is warm and genial. The prevailing winds during nine months of the year are the cierzo or NW, and the bochorno or SE. The W, called by the Aragonese. the fagueno, is generally accompanied by abundant showers, and is extremely favourable to vegetation.— Extensive tracts of rocky sterility occur on the higher elevations; and, except in the vicinity of the rivers, or localities under artificial irrigation, where it possesses great fertility, the soil is generally parched and sandy. The agricultural productions of A., consisting chiefly in wheat, of which considerable supplies are contributed to the adjacent province of Navarre, barley, oats, rye, Indian corn, pulse, flax and hemp, fruits,inclusive of grapes and olives of the finest quality,barilla, madder, saffron, Glycyrhiza glabra, or the liquorice plant, and sumach, exceed in quantity the ARA consumption of the prov. The higher districts present the richest pasturages, while the mountain-acclivities are covered to a great height with forests of beech, oaks of various species, pines, &c., affording excellent timber, and enclosing immense quantities of game. The mineral productions of A. are extremely rich; and its gold and silver mines attracted the attention of the Romans. Of the latter some traces are still to be found. Copper, lead, and iron are very abundant. Cobalt, quicksilver, jet, jasper, marble, and copperas also exist in considerable quantities. A productive alum-mine exists in the vicinity of Alcaniz; an extensive coal-field in the neighbourhood of Caspe; and a bed of rock-salt at the village of Remolinos to the NNW of Alagon, which supplies both A. and Catalonia with that commodity. The soil of A. is, to a considerable degree, impregnated with salt, and many of its rivers are of a saline quality. Fuci and other marine plants are found in profusion in the large lake of Gallocanta, in the SW of the prov. Thermal springs are numerous in the mountain-districts. A species of peat earth, which forms an excellent article of fuel, is abundant in the district of Teruel.—This prov., although it possesses no peculiar varieties, is remarkable for the number of wild animals with which its mountainous districts are stored. The bear, wolf, and lynx inhabit the forests of the Pyrenees. Sheep, of the Merino species, the wool of which constitutes the principal commercial wealth of the prov., are reared in great numbers in the lower pasturages. The supply of horned cattle and horses is to a considerable extent imported from the adjacent provinces. The rivers abound with excellent fish, particularly trout; and a much esteemed species of eel is found in a small lake near Alcaniz.—The industry of the inhabitants of this prov. is very limited, consisting principally in the manufacture of common cloths, ratteens, sack-cloth, cordage, pottery, paper, leather, brandy, wine and oil. The two latter articles are usually of excellent quality; and are produced in quantities generally exceeding the local demand. Some fine woollens are made at Jaca. A few iron-foundries exist in the vicinity of the mountain-forests. The most recent estimate of exports from this prov. amounts to £230,000, derived chiefly from wool.—The Canal Imperial de Aragon was begun in 1529 by order of Charles V., but the scheme was abandoned soon after, until near the end of the 18th century. It begins 3 m. ESE of Tudela, up to which point the Ebro is navigable; runs ESE; and passing near Saragossa, joins the Ebro again 10 leagues below. It was originally contemplated to carry it to Sastago, 40 m. SE of Saragossa. It admits vessels of from 60 to 80 tons. Several main roads, connecting the principal towns, cross the prov., but by none of these has any regular passage yet been effected over the Pyrenean chain within the limits of A. This prov. is divided into 13 corregimentos or districts, named after their chief towns: Jaca, Huesca, Benavarra, Barbastro, Alcaniz, Cincovillas, Teruel, Albarracin, Daroca, Calatayud, Borja, Tarragona, and Saragossa; containing 12 cities, of which Saragossa, or Zaragosa, is the capital, 239 villages, and 688 hamlets. It comprises 1 archbishopric, 6 bishoprics, 1,396 parishes, 8 collegiate churches, 228 convents, 2 universities, 23 hospitals, and 29 military commanderies.— The pop. presents, in relation to the extent of the prov., the disproportion commonly to be observed in Spain, being, according to the census in 1803, no greater than 657,376, and consequently affording only the proportion of 45 to a sq. m. In 1833, the pop. had only increased to 734,685. In fact this prov. is one of the least susceptible of improvement in all Spain; and this appears to have been early and accomplisation of the second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second felt, for in the preamble to one of their fueros, or ancient laws, it was declared that such was the barrenness of the country, that were it not for the political liberty its inhabitants enjoyed, the people would abandon it.—The name of this prov. is commonly supposed to be derived from that of the Roman prov. Tarraconensis. The comparative insignificance of the river of the same name, by which its NW districts are intersected, rendering the hypothesis that it originated the appellation highly improbable. The kingdom of A. originally comprised the territory of the ancient Celtiberes. In 470 it passed from the domination of the Romans to that of the Goths. In 714 it was conquered by the Moors, and was subsequently governed by sovereigns of its own until its junction with the kingdoms of Leon and Castile by the marriage of Ferdinand and Isabella. It retained for some time after its incorporation with Castile, its fueros or constitution, which had been confirmed by the Pope, its states, council, and other peculiar rights; and was governed by a viceroy who enjoyed all the external honours of a king, but these privileges were gradually withdrawn, and at last entirely abrogated.
ARAGONA, a village and district of Sicily, 8 m. N of Girgenti, near the clay volcano of Maccaluba.

ARAGUA, a fine valley in the prov. of Caraccas, in Venezuela, forming a narrow basin between granitic and calcareous mountains of unequal height. It is separated from the coast, on the N, by the Sierra Mariara; and on the S, from the steppes, by the chain of Guacimo. The streams which descend from the mountains on the E and W are collected in this valley, in the beautiful lake of Valencia or Tacarigua, at an alt. of 1,300 ft. above sea-level. When Humboldt visited this district, in 1800, it was supposed to contain above 52,000 inhabitants, on a space 13 leagues in length and 5 in breadth, making 2,000 to a square league, which is almost equal to the densest pop. of France. It was greatly desolated, however, during the war of independence.

ARAGUAYA, a large river of Brazil which rises in about S lat. 19°, near to the Parana-which runs from N to S, whilst the A. runs through the heart of Brazil from S to N-to about S lat. 6° where it joins the Tocantin. The united stream, after a course of 370 leagues, falls into the southern estuary of the Amazon, in S lat. 1° 40'. The principal tributary of the A. is the Das Mortes, which joins it in lat. 12° S. Many tribes of warlike savages dwell on the banks of the A. There is, by means of it, an uninterrupted navigation from the city of Pará to the centre of Brazil, and to the prov. of Matto- Grosso. - Mawe.
ARAGUES, a town of Spain, prov. of Aragon, 13 m. NNW of Jaen, on the Aragues river, at the foot of the Pyrenees.

ARAGUI, a river of Russia in Asia, rising in Mount Caucasus, near the source of the Terek, running S, and, after a course of 60 m., in which it receives numerous streams, falling into the Kur at Tschet, 10 m. N of Teflis.

ARAIF-ON-NAKAH (JEBEL), a conspicuous conical mountain, on the great plateau of the desert, N of the peninsula of Sinai. It is composed of limestone covered with flinty pebbles. Robinson estimated its alt. at about 500 ft. above the plateau from which it rises, which itself has an alt. of from 1,200 to 1,500 ft. above sea-level.
ARAIL, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Allahabad, on the Jumna river, at its confluence with the Ganges, and opposite the city of Allahabad.
ARAILLE' (COL D'), a rugged pass in the Pyrenees, between Cauterets and the Lac de Gaube. It commands a magnificent view of the Vignemale.

ARAISH (EL), or LARACHE, a town of Marocco, ዀ ARA in N lat. 35° 12′ 50", W long. 6° 9', on an abrupt sandstone declivity, on the S bank of the Ulkus, on a point of land between the sea and that river. It is enclosed by a wall and ditch, and its sea-defences are strong; but the town itself has fallen into decay, and does not number above 4,000 inhabitants. Within the bar-which has 16 ft. water at springtides—there is a fair port for frigates, about 3 m. long, and 4 m. broad.

ARAK MOUNTAINS, the westernmost division of a range of mountains which commences in Tartary, about N lat. 42°, E long. 72°, and runs E and NE in the northern provinces of China, until it meets the Samen mountains which separate China from Russia.

ARA'KHOVA, a village of the Morea, on the southern slope of Mount Malevo, in Arcadia, near the sources of the Alpheus and the Eurotas. The ridges of Parnassus tower above the vineyards of A. on the N and E; and the Corycian cave may be best visited from this spot.
ARAKTSCHIEF, an island in S lat. 15° 51' 20", W long. 140° 50′ 50″ [Kotzebue], discovered by Bellinghausen in 1819.

ARAL (SEA, or LAKE OF), next to the Caspian, the largest inland collection of water in Asia, measuring 290 m. in its greatest length from NE to SW; and 130 m. in breadth from E to W; and containing a total superficies of about 25,000 sq. m. It is situated amid the plains of the Turkomans and Kirghiz, encircled by deserts, sandy heaths, and morasses, near the SW extremity of Independent Tartary, and is skirted on the S by the khanate of Khiva. It lies between 43° 21' and 46° 50' N lat., and 58° 7' and 61° 22' E long. Its level is 117.6 ft. above the Caspian [Humboldt], from which it is separated by an isthmus, consisting of a sandy plateau, commencing in the vicinity of the mountains of Mangojar, and terminating on the S in a vast rocky terrace, stretching in a nearly perpendicular ridge, 500 ft. in height from the gulf of Tiyuk-kara-su on the S of the Mertui-Kultuk or Dead bay of the Caspian, to the gulf of Malmichka in the Aral. On the N side likewise this Oust-Ourt, i. e. 'High plain,' presents a precipitous face or cliff. On the N and E the outline of this sea presents numerous indentations, the principal of which are: Barsutskoi bay on the NW with a cape of the same name; Sari Chaganac bay on the NE, and at the SE extremity Ujan bay. Thence to the gulf of Malmichka at the SW corner, the coast exhibits few sinuosities. The western shore presents a pretty regular convexity, skirted by the edge of the plateau, which rises in altitude towards the N. The principal rivers which discharge themselves into the Aral sea are the Syr, or Sihun [the ancient Iaxartes], and the Amu, or Oxus. The former takes its rise in one of the ramifications of the Thian-Chan, and after dividing into three great branches, distinguished by the names of the Syr-Daria, or Syr, properly so called, and the most northerly—the Kuvan, or Kuban-Daria, and the Jan-Daria, falls into the Aral on its eastern side in lat. 46°. It is said to be navigable 100 m. from its mouth. The two latter branches are of recent formation. The Amu, or Amu-Deria, generally identified with the Oxus of the ancients, is the only important tributary on the S. It rises in the lofty table-land of Pamir, flows through the vast plain of Bokhara, intersects the khanate of Khiva from SE to NW, and enters the sea of Aral near its SW extremity by two mouths. Notwithstanding the magnitude of these tributaries, this sea has no visible outlet. The Aral is studded with islands, especially at its S extremity, whence its Tartar name, Aral dinguiss, i. e., 'the Sea of isles,' a name adopted by the Iktoba Kud Loshkar Ama Kashkar Ata (Sale lake) S M Us Teheka Œ Kuyuk Kuduk Ak Kulka Ser
