Atchir Kul Chudoyar Tyky Rin Kask Djala Alta-Kuduk Er Mula Barak Den-Kera cikh 20 Gurlen Tashaus Chaabas Vali Ambar paint Diagatay Buens Yany Urgentch L Ak-Kul Kazarat Kosh Kupyr Hanki C.Kasarat CPolyan M L Burlak-Kal Bagag KHIVA 0 Pilmia Tohan Hazander A A Fullarion & Co London & Edinburch . . . . * . . . . - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - Russians also in the appellation Aral-skoé-moré. The reported largest of these islands, Barsa-Kilmez, in the northern part of the lake it is reported by Captain Romanoff has no existence [Geog. Journ., vol. xiii., p. ] 276]. The southern area is covered to a great extent with floating islands, richly cultivated, and accessible from the land, on horseback, when the water is low. On its N side, where it is extremely shallow, the shore is covered with a forest of reeds which attain a height of 15, 20, and even 30 ft. These reeds growing up in large clumps, in the shallow water, collect the drifted sand around them, and in process of time an island is thus formed. Some naturalists are of opinion that the decrease of the waters of the Aral and Caspian is due even in a greater degree to the consumption occasioned by these aquatic plants than to ordinary evaporation. The water of the Aral is salt, and possesses the noxious characteristics of the Caspian, notwithstanding the comparatively large proportion of fresh water which it receives. It is sometimes, though not generally, frozen in winter. On the Oust-Ourt—the northern portion of the isthmus-the thermometer sometimes falls to 35° below zero of Fahrenheit, an intensity of cold nowhere surpassed in Europe; while in summer the heat on the steppes is often intense, the therm. indicating 108° in the shade, and 144° in the sun. The fish which it contains are of the same species found in the Caspian, and are taken in considerable quantities by the Kara- Kalpaks who navigate the lake in small boats.—Conflicting opinions exist with respect to the formation of the Aral sea. The evident encroachment of the sands upon its western shores; the gradual disappearance of lakes and rivers in the adjacent districts of Asia, especially towards the W; together with the absence of any authentic historical evidence of the Aral in distinction from the Caspian, previous to the 18th century, concur in supporting the hypothesis of its original union with the Caspian sea; and the sea thus formed may have stretched towards the NE, in which direction a chain of lakes and morasses extends to the swamps of Barabinsk in Siberia. On the other hand, it is contended that from the moment in which the Oust-Ourt and the Mongojar extremity of the Aral were raised to their present elevation, no river could possibly flow into the Caspian from the Aral; but that this lake has been formed by the Syr and the Oxus.— Pallas's Travels.—Engelhardt and Parrot.—Leochine, Descript. des Hordes et des Steppes des Kirghiz-Kazaks. Paris, 1840.—Zimmermann's Memoir on the Countries about the Caspian and Aral. London, 1840.—Eichwald, Alte Geographie der Kaspischen Meeres, &c. Berlin, 1838.

ARALES, or Aralians, a nation of Independent Tartary, inhabiting the plains along the banks of the Sihun, and the southern shores of Lake Aral. It consists of Usbek-Tartars, Turcomans, and Kara Kalpaks, amounting in all to 100,000 men. In summer they dwell in tents; in winter they assemble in an immense camp, fortified with earthen ramparts. They speak the Turkish language, and profess Islamism. They grow some cotton, but principally subsist by rearing vast numbers of cattle. ARAM. a town of Arabia, in Yemen, 46 m. NE of Chamir.
ARAMAGHANEH, a fortified town of Persia, in the prov. of Azerbijan, district of Hamje.
ARAMAKUTAN, one of the Kurile group of islands, in N lat. 49° 35'.
ARAMAYKA, a settlement on the Massaruni river, in Demerara, 250 m. from its mouth. The river is here a mile wide.
ARAMAYONA, a valley of Alava in Spain, on the frontiers of Bilboa, celebrated for its iron-works.
ARAMBO (JEBEL), a large solitary hill about 4 m. from the Nile, which marks the coterminous frontiers of the Nubian district of Mahass and Dongola.
ARAMITZ, a commune and town of France, dep. | Transylvania, which rise in Mount Kalimyasza, unite ARA of the Lower Pyrenees, 23 m. SW of Pau. Pop. 1,264. -Also a cant. comprising 6 communes.
ARAMO, a town of Chili, 30 m. S of La Conception.
ARAMON, a canton, commune, and town of France, on the Rhone, in the dep. of Gard, 12 m. E by N of Nismes. Pop. of com. 2,447; of cant. 11,483.
ARAMONTANE, one of the Kurile group of islands, in N lat. 49° 5'.
ARAMPALI, a town of Madura, in Hindostan. Its manufactures some years ago gave employment to 2,000 looms.
ARAN, a river of France, in the dep. of Basses- Pyrénées, flowing into the Adour, 7 m. below Bayonne, after a course of about 20 m.

ARAN, a valley of the Pyrenees, bounded on the N by the French dep. of Haute-Garonne and Ariège; on the SW by Aragon; on the S and SE by Catalonia. It consists chiefly of wood and pasture-land, with scarcely any arable surface. Potatoes and rye are the only produce, and of these scarcely enough are grown for six months' supply; so that when winter commences a great part of the population quit the valley, and enter France, where they live either by labour or begging, till the return of spring. Pop. in 1833, 6,578, principally herdsmen, woodcutters, and contrabandistos. The rental of the whole valley does not much exceed £5,000 English, one-tenth of which amount is paid in taxes into the public treasury. This valley forms a partido or administrative division of Lerida. The Noguera and the Garonne, flowing in opposite directions, one into Spain, and the other into France, have their sources here, and within 50 yards of each other.
ARANC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ain, cant. of Hauteville. Pop. 1,232.
ARANCADIAGA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 9 m. SW of Bilbao. There are iron-works here, and mineral springs.
ARANÇAY, a town of Peru, in the prov. of Truxillo, near the r. bank of the Lauricocha, in Slat. 9° 20'.
ARANDA DE DUERO, a town and partidos of Spain, in the prov. of Burgos, on the road from Burgos to Madrid, 35 m. from the former, and 90 m. from the latter. Pop. 3,800.
ARANDA DE EBRO, a town and a castle, in Aragon, on the river Xalon, 19 m. NW of Catalayud.
ARANDAS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ain, cant. of Saint-Rambert. Pop. 1,176.
ARANHA, a town of Portugal, in Estremadura, 12 m. NE of Leiria.—Also a town in the prov. of Alentejo, 10 m. SSW of Port Alegre.

ARANJUEZ, a town of Toledo, 20 m. S of Madrid, on the l. bank of the Tagus, at an alt. of 1,701 ft. above sea-level. Originally an insignificant village, it now consists of several handsome streets and public edifices. The Court residing here several months in the year has given it this impulse. The royal palace, founded by Philip II., and completed by Ferdinand VI., is a magnificent combination of art and taste; and is particularly remarkable for the beauty of its gardens and pleasure-grounds. The main road from this place to Madrid was formed by Ferdinand VI. on the plan of the ancient Roman roads. Each mile is said to have cost about £32,250. A railroad is in course of construction from A. to the cap. A portion of it extending from A. to Monte de la Reyna, a distance of 8 m., was opened on the 11th Sept. 1850.—The ordinary pop. of A. is 4,500; but during the residence of the Court it amounts to 10,000.
ARANTAC, a seaport of Peru, 30 m. SW of Arequipa, in S lat. 16° 53'. The harbour is deep; but the entrance is so narrow that it is little frequented.
ARANYOS (GREAT and LITTLE), two rivers in * near Topanfalva, and fall into the Maros at St. Martin.

ARANYOS-SZEK, a small administrative division of Transylvania, watered by the above streams. Its area is about 20 sq. m.; and it comprehends 1 town and 17 villages. Pop. in 1776, 4,173, exclusive of Wallachians; in 1833, 19,547. Its productions are grain, fruits, wine, and cattle.

ARAPAOA, an island of New Zealand, in Queen Charlotte's sound. It is a narrow strip of a very hilly character throughout its whole extent, intersected by ravines, and covered with wood. Towards the channel it forms several small coves which are inhabited; towards Cook's straits the shores are bold and rocky.—Dieffenbach.
ARAPARES, a chain of mountains in Brazil, in which the rivers Jurneno, Arinos, Paraguay, and Cuyaba take their rise.
ARAPARIPUCU, a town of Brazil, 170 m. WSW of Para, on a branch of the Maranon. ARAPATAK, [GERM. Arndorf,] a town of Transylvania, 10 m. NE of Kronstadt, near the r. bank of the Aluta.
ARAPIJO, a town of Para, in Brazil, 18 m. WSW of Gurupu.

ARAPILES, a village of Spain, 41 m. S of Salamanca, so called from two knolls, the Arapiles, about 15 m. to the E, one of which was occupied by the French, the other by the British, at the commencement of the battle of Salamanca, or of Arapiles as it is called by the French.

ARAPILES (Mount), a hilly mass in Australia Felix, in S lat. 36° 42′, and nearly on the 142d meridian. It consists of sandstone passing into quartz, and occupies a space of about 2 sq. m. From its summit Major Mitchell counted 27 circular lakes, above the surface of one of which the alt. of Mount A. was 726 ft.—Mitchell.
ARAQUI, a river of Georgia, which, rising near the Gates of Caucasus, flows to the S, and falls into the Kur near Tsgetta, 25 m. above Tiflis. It conveys a considerable body of water to the Kur.
ARAQUIL, a town of Navarre, in Spain, 16 m. W of Pampeluna, on a river of the same name.
ARARAHY, an island in the Tocantin river, in the prov. of Para, Brazil, 15 m. NNE of Villa-Vicoza. It is 9 m. in length, narrow and flat.

ARARANGUA, a river of Brazil, falling into the sea about 50 m. to the SW of San Antonio de Laguna. It has a course of about 60 m., and is navigable 15 m. from its mouth, as far up as Tres Portos, where it is 600 ft. wide.

ARARAS (SERRA D'), a range of mountains in Brazil, forming part of the boundary between the prov. of Minas-Geraes and Goyaz. - Also a river which, descending from these mountains, flows into the Rio Grande in S lat. 21° 15'.

ARARAT, the Aghri Tagh, i. e., according to some, 'Painful Mount,' or according to others, 'Heavy' or 'Great Mountain' of the Turks, and Macis or Massis of the Armenians, a lofty mountain of the Armenian plateau, in the north-eastern corner of Asia Minor, in what is now Russian Armenia, remarkable as forming "the boundary-stone, as it were, of the three great empires of Russia, Turkey, and Persia," but more so as being the spot on which the ark of Noah is generally supposed to have rested during the subsidence of the waters of the Deluge. It is a mountain of great magnitude and altitude, being, at its highest point, 17,210 ft. above the level of the sea,—an elevation considerably higher than that of Mont Blanc, - while its base, 22 m. long, by 18 m. in breadth, occupies an area of 400 sq. m. Its elevation above the plain of the Aras is 14,320 ft. It is nearly midway betwixt the southern extremities of the Black sea and the Caspian, in N lat. 39° 35', E long. 44° 30', according to Morier's map; 36 geographical miles S 25° W of Eriván, and 35 due S of Echmiadzin. Parrot fixes the summit in N lat. 39° 42', E long. from Ferro 61° 55'. Though close on the line of the immense chain of Taurus, which extends from the eastern shores of the Black sea far into Asia, Mount A, stands in a measure alone, the other hills in the neighbourhood being too insignificant in size to hide its proportions, or impair the majesty of ARA its aspect. From Erivan, A. presents two peaks, and appears to be connected with a range of mountains extending towards the NW, which, though really elevated, are dwarfed by A., which looks so high and vast, that, when the air is clear, it does not seem above 6 m. distant. From Nakchivan, nearly 100 m. distant, and whence it bears N 57° W, it appears like an immense isolated cone of extreme regularity, rising out of the low valley of the Aras. [Smith.] It is seen from Azerdbijan, 132 m. E in a direct line; and even from Derbend on the Caspian, which is fully 270 m. distant! Its vast extent and elevation is such, that Colonel Cameron states that, after a journey of nearly 60 m. between Nakchivan and Erivan, it appeared to him in exactly the same spot from whence he had originally started. The subjoined view of A., by Parrot, is from Echmiadzin, which is 35 m. distant from its base, and lies 3,035 ft. above the level of the Black sea. This great mountain is divided into two immense conical masses or heads, respectively named the Great and Little A., which form distinct cones separated from each other by a broad level valley. The two cones are about 12,000 yards apart at their summits. The peak of the Great A., on the NW, is perpetually covered with snow; but the Lesser A., on the SE, which is 10,140 ft. above the plain of the Aras, or nearly 5,000 ft. lower, is clear of snow in summer. The sign of the greatest heat is when the snow has entirely left the summit of the Lesser A. At this period the cultivators of melons cut their fruit; and in general the snows of A. are used by the surrounding agriculturists as a calendar by which to regulate the sowing, planting, and reaping of their crops. [Morier.] The two mountains present an appearance of isolation; but the SW declivity of the mass is insensibly lost in the mountains of Bayazid and Diadina, which contain the sources of the Euphrates; while the NW declivity—which is about 20 m. in length—is connected with a long chain of hills which runs along the right bank of the Aras or Araxes. The declivities of the Little A. are somewhat steeper than those of the Great A.; and its form is almost perfectly conical. Parrot describes Mount A. as a mass of volcanic rocks, heaped in confused fragments upon each other, -here masses of regularly melted lava,-there cinders,-there trachytic rock in various gradations of colours, thickness, and composition, with plain marks of the agency of volcanic heat. Parrot fixes the snow-line on A. at 14,080 ft. The limit of arborescent vegetation reaches 7,800 ft. At this height, stunted birches are found. Walnut-trees, apricots, willows, and poplars, do not ascend above 6,000 ft. At 10,000 ft., the Anthemis regescens, Ziziphera media, Veronica Telephiifolia, and Centaurea pulcherrima are found. The Saxifraga muscoides reaches 12,000 or 13,000 ft. The Araxes,—the Raksi of the natives, and perhaps the Pison of Scripture, — flows round the eastern base, amidst extensive swamps, at a distance of 5 m. Numerous traditionary stories are current respecting the constant failure of all attempts to ascend Mount A., and the punishments which have overtaken presumptuous adventurers. In 1700, Tournefort, the celebrated French botanist, attempted it, but in vain. Morier imagined that it might be scaled on the side towards Bayazid, where the base is much higher than towards the Araxes, and the ascent apparently much easier; but the attempt proved abortive. Ibrahim, pasha of Bayazid, accompanied by a large party of horsemen, at the most favourable season of the year, ascended as high as he could on horseback on the Bayazid side. He had no great difficulty in crossing the snow; but when he came to the cap of ice which covers the summit, he could proceed no farther. What, however, had baffled the pasha, was accomplished by a Russian professor, Dr. Friedrich Parrot, who ascended this mountain in the year 1829. A. is inferior in elevation to the two peaks of the Elburz, measured by Winchkemfsky, and to the Holy Mountain of the Circassians, measured by Boutsovsky: but it exceeds in elevation the Mquinvari, also measured by Parrot himself, by nearly 2,000 English ft. The lower limit of perpetual snow on the Great A. was found by the same observers to be 12,816 ft. above the level of the sea, and 2,242 ft. higher than the same line on the Mquinvari; though the latter summit is only 2° 40' N of the former; and 178 toises, or 1,160 English ft. higher than it ought to be by the law of the cosines of latitude, which has been supposed to determine this limit in every parallel of latitude. A similar anomaly takes place in the case of Mquinvari itself, where the lower limit of constant snow is 1,600 ft. higher than the Pyrenees, in the same latitude. The anomaly, in the case of A., may be not unsatisfactorily solved by considering its situation, on a lofty and extensive plateau reaching from the valley of the Tigris to that of the Kur, and from the borders of the Black sea to those of the Caspian, and where, consequently, the radiation of heat must be greater than either in the Caucasian isthmus, or in the Pyrenees, and the lower limit of constant congelation, from this circumstance, be proportionally higher. The summit of Mount A. is a slightly convex and almost circular platform, about 200 ft. in diameter, from the edges of which there is a steep declivity on all sides, forming a silver crest, composed of enduring ice and unbroken by a rock or stone. The view of the valleys and regions around the base of A.—when Dr. Parrot stood on its summit-was obscured by a grey mist; and the hills in the neighbourhood appeared like dark spots shot up through it; Erivan and Sardarabad seemed like two specks; and Lake Gotschai was visible in the far NE. The top of Little A., bearing ESE, presented the appearance of a square truncated pyramid. The land of Ararat mentioned in Scripture, it is pretty generally agreed, was some portion of the country now known under the name Armenia; but Beke and some others are of opinion that the mountains on which the ark rested must have been situate somewhere within the basin of the Euphrates, and to the S of that river. "Two objections," say the authors of Missionary Researches in Armenia, " are made to the supposition that Scripture refers to this mountain when it speaks of 'the mountains of Ararat.' One is, that there are now no olive-trees in its vicinity, from which Noah's dove could have plucked her leaf. And it is true-so far as we could learn—that that tree exists neither in the valley of the Kur nor of the Aras, nor on the coast of the Caspian, nor anywhere nearer than Batum, and other parts of the eastern coast of the Black sea,—a distance of seven days' journey of a caravan, or about 130 m. in the circuitous route that would thus be taken. But might not a dove make this journey in a day? Or might not the climate then have been warmer than it is now? The second objection is drawn from the fact, that some of the old versions and paraphrases, particularly the Chaldee and the Syriac, refer 'the mountains of Ararat' to the mountains of Kurdistan, where there is, not far from Jezeereh, a high mountain called Judi, on which the Moslems suppose the ark to have rested. But if the ark rested on that, the posterity of Noah would most likely have descended at once into Mesopotamia, and have reached Shinar from the N; while, from the valley of the Aras, they would naturally have kept along on the E side of the mountains of Media, until they reached the neighbourhood of Hamadán or Kermansháh, which is nearly E of Babylon. Such is the route now taken every day by all the caravans from this region to Bagdad." An earthquake, which changed in a few moments the entire aspect of the country in the neighbourhood of Mount A., commenced on the 20th of June (Russian, or old style), 1840, at about 45 minutes after 6 P. M. Repeated but intermittent shocks, which seemed to come from the mountain, gave to the earth a movement resembling waves, which continued for about two minutes. The first four and the most formidable shocks--which were accompanied by a subterranean sound-proceeding in the direction of ENE, have left on the summits of hills and bottoms of valleys within the range of their agitation, traces which will not soon disappear, and which the eye of the scientific observer will recognise after many ages shall have passed away. It was at the same time observed, that numerous rents or fissures took place on the banks of the Araxes and the Karasu, from the source of the latter to its confluence with the Arpachai, on all the spots where the banks of those rivers are somewhat elevated. These fissures— which were 10 or 12 ft. wide, and parallel to the course of the streams—ploughed the earth to the distance of a verst from the beds of the rivers, and, in accordance with the movement given to the soil by the shocks, they were seen every moment to open and shut. A number of violent explosions also took place from the bottoms of holes like little craters, which, opening and shutting in the same way as the fissures, spouted out torrents of water, and cast up immense quantities of pebbles and gravel. The waters of the Araxes were so violently agitated that they rose over both banks, now causing inundation, then sinking again into the centre of their bed. According to the accounts of the people of the country, several parts of the river became dry; while in other places the body of the stream rose to a great height, making a noise like the sound of boiling water. During these awful moments terror and desolation reigned to a great distance around Mount A. The Persian town of Maku, and Bayazid, the chief town of a Turkish pashalik, also suffered from the earthquake, whose successive shocks convulsed the earth as far as Shusa and Tabriz on the one side, and Tiflis on the other; but its ravages extended chiefly over the Russian territory. The ancient and venerable monastery of St. James, and the village of Arghuri or Acorhi, with its 200 houses and 1,000 inhabitants, situated on the r. bank of the Arghuri rivulet, at an alt. of 6,350 ft. above sea-level, at the foot of the Great A., were entirely destroyed by the immense masses of rock which were detached from the summit of the mountain, and by the colossal glaciers, accumulated during an incalculable series of ages in that region of eternal snow, which those rocks brought with them in their descent. In the cantons of Erivan, Sharur, Nakchivan, and Ordubat, nearly all the houses were entirely destroyed. The influence of the earthquake on all the wells within the districts of Erivan and Nakchivan was very remarkable. In the canton of Nakchivan upwards of thirty springs were dried up for some time; and some continued even several days after the catastrophe to yield only thick and whitish-coloured water; others, on the contrary, became more abundant than they had previously been. It is difficult to afford any idea of the scene which presented itself in the narrow valley of Arghuri. The masses of rock, ice, and snow, detached by the first shock from the summit of the A. and its lateral points, were thrown, at one single bound, from a height of 6,000 ft. to the bottom of the valley, where they # ARA lay scattered over an extent of seven versts Dr. Parrot is of opinion that at a period, probably anterior to all history, a similar fall must have taken place on the NNE side of the mountain, where an immense cavern extends from the snow-line downwards to a depth of 800 toises. Authorities.] Tournefort, Relation d'un Voyage du Levant.—Morier's Second Journey through Persia.— Ker Porter's Travels.—Missionary Researches in Armenia. By Smith and Dwight.—Beke's Origines Biblica.—Parrot's Journey to Ararat, in Cooley's translation. Lond., 1845, 8vo.
ARARAT, or PILOT MOUNTAIN, a lofty mountain in North Carolina, U.S., 16 m. N of Salem. Its form is that of a pyramid.
ARARIBA, a river of Brazil, which, rising in the mountains to the W of Tamaraca, runs S, and falls into the ocean near Pernambuco.

ARARIPE (SERRA), a chain of mountains in Brazil, between Serra Cayriris and Serra Boborema, in about S lat. 7° 20', W long. 39° 30', forming the boundary between the provinces of Pernambuco and Seara. Several considerable rivers take their rise in this range.

ARARUAMA, or IRUAMA, a lagune of Brazil, in the prov. of Rio de Janeiro, extending 18 m. E and W, and 10 m. across at its widest part. A reef separates it from the sea, with which it communicates by an opening 50 yards wide, about 3 m. N of Cape Frio. Several rivers run into it. Great quantities of salt are made on its shores, and it abounds in fish. On its N shore is the village of San Pedro; on the S bank of the strait which unites it with the ocean is the town of Cabo Frio.
ARAS. See ARAXES.
ARAS, or Eris, a town of Russia, in Shirvan, 50 m. N of Shamaghie, and 130 SSE of Teflis.
ARASCHA, a river of Mingrelia, which rises near the village of Kemme, and uniting with the Hippus, divides Mingrelia from Iberia, and enters the Phasis or Rion near the Tredia.—Kinneir.

ARASSIE, a maritime and trading town of Piedmont, in the territory of Genoa, 5 m. SW of Albenga, in N lat. 44° 4', E long. 8°. It has a good harbour; and travellers frequently hire vessels at this place for different parts of Italy.
ARATHAPESCOU. See ATHABASCA.
ARATICA, or Carlshoff, an island in the Paumotu group, 8 m. in length by 5 m. in breadth. Its W point is in S lat. 15° 26', W long. 145° 39' 46".
ARATICU, a river of Brazil, running N between the Jucunda and Tocantin, and falling into the Tapijura.
ARATURA, a river rising in the mountains of Itamaca, and falling into the sea a little to the S of the Orinoco. It is navigable 30 m., and its banks are covered with fine timber.
ARAU. See AARAU.

ARAUCANIANS, or ARAUCANOS, a numerous, warlike, and independent tribe of S America, who inhabit a district comprising an area of 64,000 sq. m., in the southern part of Chili, lying between the Andes and the Pacific, and stretching from the Biobio on the N, to the Valdivia on the S, a distance of 186 m., embracing the finest plains in the whole country. They are of middle stature; their complexion is an olive-brown; their heads are large; their faces round; their eyes small, but horizontally placed; their foreheads narrow; their chins broad and short; their hair is black, and hangs loosely over their shoulders. Like the Tartars, they have little or no beard. Their dress consists of a shirt, a vest, a pair of short close drawers, and a large cloak, with an opening in the middle for the head, and made full and long so as to cover the hands and descend to the knees. On their heads they wear a bandage of embroidered wool. Their general expression is good-natured and lively. This people, although but little advanced in the arts of civilized life, are represented as the most cultivated of the indigenous races of this vast continent. In character they are described as brave, and passionately addicted to national predilections, designating themselves, in the pride of patriotism, Ché-reche, i. e., 'the pure na tion.' They are generous and hospitable to members of their own tribe, whom they address by the name of penni (brother), but punctilious and irrascible, cruel and vindictive, towards their enemies, and insatiably addicted to predatory warfare. Their bravery has always preserved them from Spanish rule. Army after army which has crossed the Biobio for the subjugation of their territory, has fallen by the war-club or spear, or been compelled hastily to retrace its steps across that river. They are temperate in diet, rarely using either meat or fish, although the rivers and coasts of A. abound with the latter. Their principal food is farinaceous, and chiefly consists of maize and potatoes; and their ordinary beverage is a species of cyder which they prepare from maize, honey, and various kinds of fruit. They are rarely guilty of excess in drinking, except on occasions of marriages or funerals, when their banquets are generally prolonged to two or three days. They are an athletic race, and are early subjected by their parents to a rigorous physical education. Their moral discipline is said to be proportionably severe; but all species of juvenile punishment is disallowed, on the supposition that it renders the character pusillanimous and cowardly. Polygamy is admitted by the admapu, or national code; and its practice is only limited by their means of purchase, a specific dowry being required by the parents of each wife. The first wife only is considered legitimate; all the others are called inandomo, or secondary wives. The marriage offering of the woman consists of a complete suit of wearing apparel, including the poncho, or scapulary-shaped mantle, which forms one of the most important articles of the commerce of A. with the Spaniards of the surrounding and adjacent provinces. Each wife is also expected to present her husband daily with a dish prepared on her own fire—the fires in each house being always equal in number to that of its female inhabitants. Treason, assassination, adultery, theft, and sorcery, are capitally punished by the jurisprudence of Araucania: minor offences are explated by pecuniary compensation. The territory of A. is divided into 4 uthanmapu, or principalities, of nearly equal extent; each of which is subdivided into five aillereques, or provinces, and each prov. into nine rehues, or counties. The A. never form towns, but live in scattered villages or hamlets on the banks of rivers, or on plains that are easily irrigated; and each family prefers to live upon the land inherited from its ancestors. The government is an aristocratic republic. It consists of three orders of magistrates, corresponding in their respective number to that of the divisions and subdivisions of the territory. The highest are the toquis, or judges, each of whom exercises in his respective uthanmapus, or tetrarchy, an entirely independent authority. These, as well as the offices of the subordinate governors, named apo-ulmenes and ulmenes, are hereditary in the male line. On occasions of general interest, a diet consisting of all the functionaries of the state is convened; and in the case of war, a generalissimo is selected from their number who is invested with absolute power, and who levies the federal contingent of men from the various provinces. The lieutenant-general is usually taken from the Reelches-an independent tribe of Araucanians, who inhabit the Pine-mapu, or Andean section of the confederation. The military force of this people consists of both cavalry and infantry—regular bodies of the former having been organised on the mode of the Spanish squadrons as early as 1585. They wear no uniform; their only military insignia are cuirasses and helmets made of indurated leather. The infantry are divided into regiments of 1,000 men, and companies of 100, each bearing a banner decorated with a star. Their arms are bows and arrows, and pikes and clubs pointed with iron; those of the cavalry, swords and lances. Each soldier is provided, for his sustenance, with a bag of prepared rye. Their military tactics evince considerable skill, and are practised with great adroitness and intrepidity. When pursued, they escape shots and sabre-strokes by the dexterity with which they fling themselves on either side of their horses: sometimes even hanging under the horse's belly while it is at full gallop. Treaties of peace are negotiated by a a deputy from each of the uthanmapus, and their ratification consists in the sacrifice of several lamas, and in partaking with the agent of the adverse party in a common repast. The A. believe in the existence of a supreme divinity, creator of all things-whom they designate by a variety of names descriptive of his various attributes. They also do homage to several subordinate deities of both sexes, whom they regard as vicegerents of the supreme deity. They have neither temples nor priests, and are more addicted to the baser practices of superstition, such as sorcery, than imbued with devotion. They believe in the immortality of the soul, which, in contradistinction from the materiality of the body, they call auc or pulle, i. e., 'spirit,' and conceive that after death it goes to a region beyond the sea, either to enjoy eternal blessedness or to expiate the crimes committed in the present life. They determine the solstices with great accuracy, by observations of the sun's shadow. Their solar year commences on the 22d December, and is divided into 11 months of 30 days, and 1 of 35. They divide it also into four seasons, each consisting of an equal number of months. They calculate time from midnight, and divide the day and night into 12 parts, distinguished by different names. They hold the art of oratory in high esteem, and regard its attainment indispensable to succession to the office of an ulmene; and possess an order of poets by whom the actions of their heroes are celebrated.—The chief articles of import into Araucania, are horse-furniture, cutlery, spirituous liquors, grain, and wine. Those of export are ponchos or cloaks, and some triffing manufactures of feathers and basket-work.- Molina.

ARAUCO, a fortress of Chili, in a valley near the mouth of a river to which it gives name, 25 m. <sup></sup> ARA S of La Concepcion. It was constructed to restrain the incursions of the Araucanian Indians.
ARAULES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Loire, cant. of Yssengeaux. Pop. 1,914.

ARAURE, a city of Venezuela, on the Acarigua, 15 m. NNE of Truxillo, originally founded by the Capuchin missionaries. The inhabitants employ themselves in the rearing of cattle, and cultivating cotton and coffee.

ARA'VULLI, or ARAVALI, a mountain-range in Northern India, extending from about 24° N lat. and 73° E long., to 28° N lat. and 76° E long.; or from the district of Edur N of Ahmednuggur, on the head-streams of the Suhurmuddy, to the frontier Jeypur town of Narnol, 86 m. SW from Delhi, on the Nansauti, which, descending from this extremity of the A., unites with the Jumna above Delhi, Its general direction is NNE and SSW, along a line of nearly 300 m., all within the Rajasthan territory. Its breadth varies from 60 m. to 6 m. To the S of the peak of Komulmair in N lat. 25° 10', E long. 73° 35' [alt. 3,353 ft.], it forms a broad mass of mountainous country, composed of numerous high ridges running SW and NE. From Komulmair, N to Ajmeer, in N lat. 26° 19', a space of about 90 m. in length, it forms a compact range of table-land, varying in breadth from 6 to 20 m. At Ajmeer it breaks into several lofty ridges, one of which sweeps around in an ENE direction, and passing to the N of Jeypur, runs N to Narnol. At its southern extremity this chain is connected with the Vindhya range, which runs parallel with the Nerbudda, by two or three long ranges, whose general direction is from NW by N to SE by S. The isolated peak of Mount Abu is separated from the western base of the A., in the parallel of 24° 40', by a wide valley, whose lowest level bears an elevation of 2,000 ft. above the sea. This mountain, which geognostically belongs to the A. range, has an alt. of 5,000 ft.; and the subjoined diagram exhibits a section of the country from Abu (A) across the Aravali (B) to Udipur (C), which is on the same level with the base of Abu, or 2,000 ft. above the sea. The average height of the A. range is only about 3,000 ft.; but its declivity to the W is very steep, and nearly the whole country on this side is a sandy waste, extending westward to the valley of the Indus. and southward to the head of the gulfs of Cambay and of Cutch, so that this side of India may be regarded as impracticable to any invading enemy. An extensive table-land, forming three distinct terraces, stretches from the eastern base of the A. to the Sindh. The A. range is of primitive formation. It exhibits granite reposing on a massive dark blue slate. The internal valleys abound in variegated quartz and schistus slate of various hues; and the diverging ridges W of Ajmeer exhibit summits " quite dazzling with the enormous masses of vitreous rose-coloured quartz." That part of the A. to the S of Komulmair is in the possession of a number of independent tribes governed by chiefs called Racouts. The northern and narrower part of the chain, called Mairwarra, i. e., 'the Region of hills,' is inhabited by a race called Mair, whose numerous villages and hamlets are scattered over the rocks and valleys, amongst which they cultivate the scanty soil with considerable industry and skill. The A. and its subordinate hills are rich both in mineral and metallic products.-Tod's Rajasthan.

ARAWAAKS, an Indian tribe in British Guayana; the most civilized of the native races in that country. They are seldom more than 5 ft. 4 in. in height, and are stoutly formed. Like all the neighbouring tribes, their animal perceptions are astonishingly acute, and render them particularly serviceable in traversing the intersected country and swamps adjoining the plantations.

ARAWAN (EL), a commercial town in the Sahara, 120 m. N by W of Timbuctu. It possesses no resources of its own, but is the point of rendezvous for caravans from Tafilet, Mogador, Drah, Tawat, Aghadem, and Tripoli. The town consists of about 500 houses, with an average of 6 inhabitants to each. With the exception of the slave part of the pop., the inhabitants are all Moors, but they pay tribute to the Tuaricks. The streets are wider than those of Timbuctu, but the houses are inferior. The surrounding country is quite parched and barren: as far as the eye can reach no trace of vegetation is perceptible. -Caillie.
ARAXA, a river of Spain, which joins the Orio at Tolosa.

ARAXES, or Aras, a large river of Armenia. It has two sources, -one to the W, and another to the The former rises on the eastern side of the S. Aggi-Dagh, by which it is divided from the source of the Elijak branch of the Euphrates—and runs E to Hassan-Kaleh, and thence to Kupri-kieu, where it is joined by the southern branch. At this confluence the river is 50 yards wide; and is crossed by a handsome stone-bridge of 7 arches. The southern branch rises in a culminating point of the mountains, about 20 m. SE of Erzerum, whence the Lycus runs to the NW, the river of Khanus to the SE, and this branch of the A. to the NE, under the name of the Tatusu. From hence it flows due S to the village of Tatus, which is 28 m. SE of Erzerum. At a point 9 m. beyond this it is 30 yards broad, and so deep that horses may swim in it. Two miles beyond this, it turns to the NE, runs through the mountains, enters the plain at the village of Yaghan 3 m. to the S of the road leading west to Erzerum, and joins the western branch at the place above stated. It is of this southern branch, and the Lycus-which is separated from it merely by an intervening ridgethat Pliny speaks, when he says that the Euphrates and A. spring at the distance of only 6 m. from each other. This is exactly predicable of the southern branch of the northern Euphrates, and the southern branch of the A.; but not of the Elijak branch, and that of Hassan-Kaleh. From this confluence of its head-streams the A. runs almost due E through the elevated plateau of Western Armenia till it receives here. on the left the Harpasus, or Arpashai, a stream equal in volume to itself, coming from the N. In Morier's opinion the Arpashai is the main branch of the A., which is not a considerable river till joined by this stream, 2 m. below Hadji Biramlu, 51 m. W of Erivan, and half that distance E of Kars. At this point, the Arpashai, when crossed by Morier in November, was 100 yards broad, very rapid, and excessively cold. It was doubtless here that the 10,000 Greeks crossed it under Xenophon, who, in his Anabasis, makes it 400 feet broad. It must have been below the junction of the river of Kars and the Arpashai that the 10,000 Greeks passed; and not, as Rennel says, at or near the place where stand the ruins of Anni, where the stream could not possibly be of the magnitude affirmed by Xenophon, as these ruins are above the junction of the Arpashai with the river from the Balagez lake. From this point the A. bends SE to Armauria or Armavir, an ancient Armenian city now in ruins, and now called Kara-Kala, or 'the ARB Black castle.' It then runs SE by the base of Mount Ararat, to its junction with the Kur, -a distance of more than 300 m. by the course of the stream. After the Arpashai, it receives successively, from the N, the Karasu, the Ashtarek, the Sanga or Zengui from the lake of Sevanga, the Gurni, the Eastern or Lesser Arpashai, the river of Nakchivan, the river of Megri, and the Kapanekshai; whilst on the S side it receives the Akurlu, originating in the snows of Ararat, the Otur, or river of Khoy, the river of Marant, and the double river of Abhar and Ardevil, from Azerbeidjan. The whole course of the A. from its source near Tatus, is 510 m. measured on Morier's map of Azerbeidjan; namely, 200 m. to its junction with the Arpashai, and 310 m. from that point to its junction with the Kur. The united stream first runs 30 m. E, and then bends S, and enters the Caspian, by three mouths, in about N lat. 39° 20'. Below the ruined town of Julfa, in N lat. 38° 54', a branch from the Childir-Dagh crosses the A., joins the mountains of Azerbeidjan, and obstructs the passage of the river. The A. seems to have forced its way through this vast range of mountains, and flows here with great rapidity. In one place Morier found the descent to be 20 ft. in 500 yards. Its channel is much impeded with rocks, and narrowed to a breadth of 30 yards; whilst the mountains on either side rise to 1,000 ft. above the stream. This river has obtained poetical celebrity for its rapidity from Virgil, whose 'pontem indignatus Araxes,' is well-known to every tyro in classical literature. But it is not quite true that the A. disdains a bridge and sweeps it off, for there are several bridges across it, some of which are very ancient; and those which have been destroyed, have been so not by the wrathful rapidity of the stream, but by the destructive agency of man. In fact, its descent is by no means very great till it enters Eastern Armenia, and flows towards the plain of Mogan.
ARAXI, a river of Paraiba, in Brazil, which runs into the Mongagnaba.
ARAY, a town of Japan, between Miaco and Yedo. No one may pass A. towards Yedo without a passport.

ARAYA (SANTIAGA DE), a cape of South America, which forms the N point of the embouchure of the Orinoco, in N lat. 10° 36'. Here salt is procured by digging pits in the clayey soil, which is impregnated with muriate of soda. The original salt-mines were destroyed by a hurricane in 1726, when a great extent of land was completely inundated, and a gulf formed several miles in length. This point was once famous for its pearl-fishery, which was gradually exhausted by the impolitic zeal of the fishers. Goats of a very fine and large breed are reared by the natives

ARAZA, a large river of Peru, which rises in the Andes of Cachao, in the prov. of Pomabamba, and dividing into various streams, enters the Amazon, after a course of 600 m., in S lat. 4°, W long. 63°. It receives also the designations of the Cuchivara, or Purus.
ARBANATS, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant. of Podensac. Pop. 479. Excellent white wines are made here.
ARBAS, a commune and village of France, dep. of Haute-Garonne, 12 m. SE of St. Gaudens. Pop. 1,138. Iron-works, glass-houses, and some other manufactories, are established here.

ARBE, or ARBA, an island in the gulf of Quarnero, in the Adriatic, between Kerso and the coast of Croatia. Pop. 5,000. It is 12 m. in length from NW to SE; and about 30 m. in circumference. Its productions are grapes, corn, figs, olives, silk, and cattle. The principal exports are wool, sheep, hides, hogs, fish, salt, fruit, cattle, and horses. Its cap. of the same name is situated on a rising ground on the S side, in N lat. 44° 46', E long. 14° 45', between two harbours forming a peninsula. Salt-pits are established here, which, with fruit and cattle, form its principal branches of trade. Pop. 1,000.—Fortis.
ARBECA, a town and castle of Catalonia, in Spain, 10 m. SE from Lerida.
ARBECEY, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Haute-Saone, cant. of Combeaufontaine. Pop. 988.
ARBEDO, a town in Switzerland, cant. of Ticino, 2 m. ENE of Bellinzona, on the Muesa. Pop. 627. A battle was fought here betwixt the Swiss and Italians on June 30th, 1422.
ARBEGEN, a town of Transylvania, 7 m. N of Stoltzenberg.
ARBELA. See Erbil.
ARBENT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ain, cant. of Oyonnax. Pop. 1,048.
ARBE'OST, a commune and v. of France, dep. of Hautes-Pyrénées, cant. of Aucun, 9 m. W of Argeles. Here are lead-mines and iron-works. Pop. 1,059.
ARBER, a mountain of Bavaria, in the circle of Lower Bavaria, near the sources of the Regen, SE of Közting. It is the culminating point of the Böhmerwald. Alt. 1,310 metres, or 4,297 ft.
ARBERG. See AARBERG.
ARBESAU, a village of Bohemia, in the circle of Leitmeritz, 1 m. NE of Kulm. Pop. 275. A battle was fought here betwixt the French and Austrians on Sept. 17th, 1813.
ARBESBACH, a town of Lower Austria, in the circle of Upper Manhartsberg, 16 m. SW of Zwetl. Here is a glass manufactory.
ARBESTAAL, a town of Austria, 5 m. N of Brugg.
ARBIA, a river of Tuscany, flowing into the Ombrone-di-Sienna, on the r. bank, at Buonconvento, after a course of 30 m.
ARBICA, a town of Navarre, in Spain, 30 m. WNW of Pampeluna.
ARBIRLOT, a parish and village on the sea-coast of Forfar. The inland parts are fertile, and there are several mineral springs in the parish. Pop. in 1821, 1,062; in 1831, 1,086; in 1841, 1,045.

ARBOGA, an ancient town of Sweden, in the prefecture of Westeraas, 65 m. W of Stockholm, on the Arboga-A or Ulvison, which is navigable to this place. By a canal, about 7 m. in length, it communicates with Orebro, through the lake of Hielmar; while the Arboga is navigable to Lake Mœlar. Near this town is a sacred grove, and some remains of Pagan worship, and other antiquities. It has a considerable trade in leather, iron, copper, saddlery, stuffs, &c. The senate of Sweden sat here during the plague in 1710. Pop. in 1810, 1,412; in 1833, 1,747.

ARBOIS, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Jura, arrond. of Poligny. The cant. comprises 14 com., and had a pop. in 1831 of 13,321. —The com. had a pop. of 6,741 in 1844. The town lies on the Cuisance in a deep gorge, but at an alt. of 940 ft. above sea-level, 18 m. NE of Lons-le-Saulnier. It is celebrated for the excellence of its wines, pulse, and flowers. It was the birth-place of Pichegru.
ARBOLETES (CIENEGA DE LOS), a port of Carthagena, on the gulf of Darien. The Punta de Arboletes is in N lat. 8° 55', W long. 76° 29½'. The coast is well-wooded and sheltered from all winds.

ARBON, an ancient town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Thurgau, on the S bank of the lake of Constance, 8 m. NE of Saint Gall, in N lat. 47° 31'. Pop. 1,130. The people are industrious, and manufacture cotton goods of all descriptions with great skill. The parish-church is the joint property of the Catholic and Protestant inhabitants of this town; and they worship in it alternately. ARC
ARBORA (CAPE), a headland on the coast of Anadoli, in N lat. 37° 21', E long. 27° 12'.
ARBORFIELD, a parish in Berks, 6 m. WSW of Wokingham. Area 1,430 acres. Pop. 300.
ARBORI, a village of Austria, circle of Czernowitz, 16 m. WNW from Sutschawa. Pop. 1,692.
ARBORIO, a town of Piedmont near the r. bank of the Sesia, 12 m. NE of Verceil.

ARBOS, a town of Catalonia, 21 m. NE of Tarragona. There are some remains of its ancient fortifications. It is situated on an eminence, in a pleasant and fertile country, commanding a magnificent prospect from the tower of the church.
ARBRA, a town of Sweden, prov. of Helsingland, 24 m. NW of Soderhamn.
ARBRE, a commune and village of Belgium, 9 m. SW of Namur. Pop. 591.

ARBRESLE (L'), or La Bresle, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Rhone, 13 m. NW of Lyons, at the junction of the Brevenne and Turdine. In 1715 the town was nearly destroyed by the overflowing of these rivers. Considerable quantities of hemp are produced here, and there is a copper-mine at Saint-Bel. Pop. of com. and t. 1,295; of cant., which comprises 17 com., in 1831, 15,863.

ARBROATH, a parish and town in Forfarshire. The p. has an area of 1,820 acres, and an extent of coast along the German ocean of about 1 m. Pop. in 1841, 8,707. The town is 17 m. E by N of Dundee, and 123 m. W by S of Montrose. It is chiefly in the p. of A.; but extends into that of St. Vigean's also. It is a place of some trade; and the revenue of its harbour in 1846 was £3,333, while the customs duties amounted to £6,572. The arrivals were 731 loaded vessels, of which 52 were from the colonies and foreign; and the aggregate tonnage was 46,080 tons. The old and new harbours have a total area of 6 acres, with a depth of 12 ft. at high-water spring tides. About 2,000 hand-looms are employed in linen weaving in the town and neighbourhood, and there are 16 mills for spinning yarn in the town and suburbs. Pop. in 1811, 9,000; in 1831, 13,705; in 1841, 14,576. Houses in 1841, 3,380. A. unites with Forfar, Montrose, Inverbervie, and Brechin, in returning a member to parliament. The parliamentary constituency in 1842-3 was 411. There is a railway from A. to Forfar <math>15\frac{1}{4}</math> m. in length, with 6 intermediate stations. It has been leased to the Aberdeen railway company, and connected with their line at Friockheim.—The venerable ruins of the abbey, founded here in 1178, still attract the attention of tourists.
ARBURG. See AARBURG.
ARBUS, a village of Sardinia, in the district of Iglesias, celebrated for its lead-mines. Pop. 2,398.— Also a commune of France, in the dep. of Basses- Pyrenees, cant. of Lescar. Pop. 837.
ARBUTHNOT, a parish in Kincardineshire, 24 m. NW of Bervie. Pop. 1,015.
ARBUTHNOT'S RANGE, a mountain ridge in Australia, intersected by the parallel of 32° 15' S, and he meridian of 148° 45' E.

ARC, a river of Savoy, rising in the Alps at Mount Iseran. It passes Lans-le-Bourg, St.-Jean-de-Maurienne, and Aiguebelle; and after a course of 75 m., falls into the Isère, 6 m. WNW of the last-named town.—Also a river of France, dep. of Bouches-du- Rhône, rising near Trets. It passes near to Aix, and, after a course of 40 m., falls into the lake of Berre.- Also a village of France, dep. of Haute-Saone, 1 m. NW of Gray. Pop. 1,494.—Also a village in the dep. of Doubs, 8 m. SW of Quingey. Pop. 1,087. Productive salt-works exist here. It is sometimes known as Arc-sous-Cicon.
ARCA, a town of Asiatic Turkey, 20 m. W of Malatia, on the r. bank of the Koremoz.-Also a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Pernambuco, on the l. bank of the Rio Francisco.

ARCACHON (Bassin D'), a bay on the SW coast of the French dep. of Gironde, at the mouth of the Leyre. From the port of Teste on this bay there is a railroad to Bordeaux. Cape Ferret, 1 m. N of the entrance of the basin, is in N lat. 44° 38' 43", W long. 1° 14′ 53". There is a fixed light on this cape, 167 ft. above water-mark.
ARCADE, a village of Lombardy, in the prov. and 9 m. NW of Treviso. Pop. 2,000.

ARC-EN-BARROIS, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Haute-Marne, arrond. of Chaumont. The cant., comprising 9 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 6,362. The com. and v. are situated on the Aujon. Pop. 1,469.
ARC-SENANS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Doubs, cant. of Montbenoit. Pop. 1,087. There is a salt-work here.
ARC-SUR-TILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Côte-d'Or, cant. of Dijon. Pop. 1,080. Marble quarries exist here.

ARCADIA, a division of ancient Greece, forming the central and highest part of the Peloponnesus; bounded on the N by Achaia; on the E by Argolis; on the S by Laconia and Messenia; and on the W by Triphylea and Elis. Its superficies amounted to about 1,600 sq. m.; and it embraced portions of the modern nomarchies of Cyllenia, Corinthia, Mantinea, and Gortynia. It is rich in rivers, springs, and pastures; and is watered by the Alpheius, and its large tributaries. The principal mountains are those of Cyllene now Zyria, Erymanthus, Stymphalus, and Mænalus. From its first inhabitants, the Pelasgi, this district derived the name Pelasgia. In later times it was divided among the sons of Lycaon, and received from his grandson, Arcas, the name Arcadia. In the course of time, its small states formed a confederacy. The principal were, Mantineia, where, B. C. 362, Epaminondas obtained a victory and a tomb, now the village of Mondi; Tegeia, now Tripolitza; Orchomenus; Pheneus; Psophis, on the Erymanthus, near the modern Tripotamo; Stymphalus; and Megalopolis.—A small seaport of Triphylia, 22 m. N of Navarino, in N lat. 37° 22', E long. 21° 34', bears the name of Arcadia; and the coast of Triphylia, and Elis, from Cape Kanella, in N lat. 37° 11′ 40″, to Cape Katakolo in N lat. 37° 28', forms the gulf of Arcadia, whose shores are generally low, but rise gently into elevated land.
